Comet Browser stands out for anyone who cares about research, productivity, or wants their browser to actually help them get things done. Its built-in assistant doesn't just fetch info, it navigates the web for you, interacts with your tabs, and even digs into your calendar and emails. If you need accurate sources, citations, or just want the browser to do the heavy lifting while you shop or research, Comet is the clear pick.

Safari, on the other hand, is all about stability and a familiar experience, especially if you live fully in the Apple ecosystem. If you want your browser to actually help you work or research, pick Comet. Only stick with Safari if you value sameness and battery life above all else. Comet is simply in a different league when it comes to features that make you more productive.

Comet Browser actually brings something new to the table with its built-in assistant that can interact with your tabs, calendar, emails, and even navigate the web for you like a real assistant. This agentic browsing isn't just a gimmick, it's genuinely useful for deep research and shopping, especially since it gives clear citations and is totally free to use in agent mode.

Shift, on the other hand, mostly just wraps your favorite apps and accounts in a sidebar with some quick switching. It doesn't really evolve much beyond what you could already do with Chrome plus a few extensions, and it's starting to feel outdated compared to newer browsers.

If you care about having an assistant that can actually help you research or take actions online, Comet is the obvious choice. Shift is only worth considering if all you want is an account switcher pinned to a sidebar, but even then, most of what it offers can be replicated in your current browser. For anyone looking for real advances in browsing, Comet is the clear winner here.

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
Comet Browser gives you a free agent mode that can handle your tabs, emails, calendar, and even navigate the web for you, while Sidekick feels outdated and its productivity features are now standard elsewhere.

Only use Sidekick if you have a specific reason, since Comet Browser is the only one here with truly free agent abilities.

Comet Browser blows SigmaOS out of the water when it comes to agentic browsing and AI-powered web navigation. Comet doesn't just add an assistant on top; it actually lets you hand off real tasks like navigating sites, searching with context, and even digging up promo codes while shopping, all for free. SigmaOS, on the other hand, is all about rethinking browser layouts and layering on productivity tweaks. But it doesn't bring anything close to the AI or agentic features that set Comet apart. If what you care about is having a browser that can actually take actions for you on the web and help with research or shopping, SigmaOS just isn't in the same league.

If your priority is AI-driven browsing where the assistant can act for you, Comet is the obvious choice. SigmaOS might be interesting for trying a fresh UI, but it can't touch Comet's agentic capabilities.

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
Comet Browser lets you use a built-in assistant to interact with tabs, emails, and your calendar or even browse for you, while Toby just gives you a nicer new tab page and basic tab grouping.

Only use Toby if you only want a lightweight way to organize tabs; choose Comet Browser if you want free access to an assistant that can actually help with research or shopping.

Comet Browser actually takes action for you by letting its built-in assistant interact with your tabs, emails, calendar, and even navigate the web on your behalf. You get a real agent experience, not just pretty organization. If you care about research, fact-checking, or want help shopping (like finding promo codes on mobile), Comet is the only one here that delivers meaningful value, especially since its agent mode is fully free.

If you need real assistance, automation, or help with complex browsing tasks, Comet Browser is miles ahead. Toby is only worth considering if you want something basic and pretty without expecting deeper help.

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
Comet Browser gives you a free assistant that can interact with your tabs, emails, calendar, and even browse the web for you, which Vivaldi does not offer.

Only use Vivaldi if you absolutely need to customize every button's placement and can't stand standard browser layouts; otherwise, pick Comet Browser.

Comet Browser stands out for its integrated assistant that can actually navigate the web for you, interact with your tabs, and even handle tasks like checking your email or finding promo codes while shopping. It's built for anyone who wants deep research or hands-off web actions, and the fact that you get agent mode for free is rare.

Vivaldi is all about customization, letting you move every button and tweak every theme, but its design feels stuck in 2015 and what used to be special is now common across browsers.

If you want a browser that actually helps you get things done or find reliable info without doing all the clicking yourself, grab Comet. Only go with Vivaldi if you absolutely need to rearrange every button and don't care about modern web helpers. For research, shopping, and letting an assistant do the work, Comet is the clear pick.

### Comet Browser vs Workona
**Live Link**: [https://efficient.app/compare/comet-vs-workona](https://efficient.app/compare/comet-vs-workona)

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
Comet Browser gives you a free agent mode with an assistant that can handle your tabs, emails, calendar, and even browse for you, while Workona just organizes tabs into workspaces in your current browser.

Only use Workona if you need simple tab management without leaving Chrome or Safari; choose Comet Browser for research or shopping with agent help.

Editor's Verdict
Editor's Verdict

Comet Browser stands out for deep research and fact-finding because its assistant is built to fetch accurate information directly from the web, complete with clear citations. If you care about getting reliable data, sources, or need an assistant to actively navigate and interact with sites for you, Comet is the one to pick. It's also the only option here that offers agentic browsing for free, so you can have the assistant do tasks like finding promo codes or summarizing emails without paying extra.

Workona, on the other hand, is all about keeping your tabs and workspaces organized within whatever browser you're already using. It's perfect if your main struggle is managing lots of tabs in Chrome or Safari and you don't want to switch browsers. But for actual research, fact-checking, or letting an AI handle web navigation, Workona doesn't compete.

If your priority is researching, shopping, or having an AI assistant do the heavy lifting online, Comet Browser is the clear choice. If you just want to tame your tab chaos in your current browser, stick with Workona. For anything that involves finding factual information or leveraging AI to interact with the web, Comet is the obvious pick.

### Comet Browser vs Zen Browser
**Live Link**: [https://efficient.app/compare/comet-vs-zen](https://efficient.app/compare/comet-vs-zen)

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
Comet Browser gives you a free agent mode that can interact with your tabs, emails, calendar, and even browse the web for you, while Zen Browser just copies Arc's sidebar and runs into bugs and extension issues because it's built on Firefox.

Only use Zen Browser if avoiding Google and Chromium matters more than having agent features or reliable site support, otherwise, stick with Comet Browser.

Comet Browser stands out because it brings real agentic browsing to the table, letting you talk to the assistant, have it interact with your tabs, emails, and calendar, and even take over web navigation like a human assistant. This isn't just surface-level AI search; it's the only browser here that actually does things for you, and you get all this for free.

Zen Browser is basically a Firefox-based Arc clone for people who want to avoid Google and Chromium, but you lose a ton of extension support and end up with more buggy website experiences. Unless you're a diehard Firefox fan who cares more about escaping Chromium than getting practical new features, there's no real advantage here.

If you care about having a browser that actually works for you and can handle deep research, shopping, or just making your life easier with AI, Comet is the obvious choice. Zen only makes sense if you're specifically loyal to Firefox and willing to accept missing extensions and a rougher experience. For everyone else, Comet is miles ahead.

### Copper vs Airtable
**Live Link**: [https://efficient.app/compare/copper-vs-airtable](https://efficient.app/compare/copper-vs-airtable)

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
We love Copper when used as a CRM (it's what we use), and we love Airtable when used as a database (not as a CRM). We actually don't recommend using Airtable as a CRM at all (Airtable is not a CRM).

If you're using Google Workspace, then Copper is the best CRM on the market due to its deep native integrations with Google, unmatched by any of the best CRMs on the market.

1
Copper
Best
Best

Best CRM for Google Workspace

Best CRM for Google Workspace
Claim deals
2
Airtable

Barebones relational contact management

Barebones relational contact management
Read review
Comparison Video
Comparison Video
Copy Link

Best Small Business CRM?

Best Small Business CRM?
3m 16s
14s
0:00
/
13:56
13:56

Best Small Business CRM?

Best Small Business CRM?
Watch
73.1K views
Editor's Verdict
Editor's Verdict
Copy Link

If you want the best Google Workspace integration, Copper is in a league of its own. You can run your entire CRM workflow inside Gmail and Calendar without switching tabs, thanks to their Chrome extension and deep email syncing. Teams relying on Google Workspace will find adoption way smoother here, since everything just shows up where you already work.

Airtable is a flexible database that's great for replacing Google Sheets or form tools, but it's not built to be a CRM and doesn't match Copper's native Google Workspace integration. If you try to force Airtable into that CRM role, you'll end up missing out on all the real-time Gmail and Calendar connections that Copper nails.

If your main need is a CRM that truly lives inside Google Workspace, Copper is the only one that delivers that experience. Airtable can't compete on this front. Pick Copper if Google integration is your priority.

### Copper vs Asana
**Live Link**: [https://efficient.app/compare/copper-vs-asana](https://efficient.app/compare/copper-vs-asana)

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
Copper lets you fully manage Gmail and Google Calendar from inside the app, which Asana cannot match if you rely on Google Workspace.

Only use Asana if you have a large team with complex needs and someone to handle setup, otherwise stick with Copper for tight Google integration.

Editor's Verdict
Editor's Verdict
Copy Link

Copper blows Asana out of the water when it comes to Google Workspace integration. Copper lets you manage your entire Gmail inbox from inside the CRM, has a Chrome extension that keeps everything inside Gmail and Calendar, and goes way deeper with email syncing than Asana even attempts. If your team lives in Google Workspace, Copper is hands down the choice for seamless integration and workflow.

Asana doesn't compete here at all. It's built for complex project management and requires a lot of setup, but it doesn't offer anything close to Copper's deep Google Workspace connectivity. If you want true, time-saving integration with Gmail, Calendar, and Drive, just go with Copper.

### Copper vs Attio
**Live Link**: [https://efficient.app/compare/copper-vs-attio](https://efficient.app/compare/copper-vs-attio)

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
Copper lets you manage your Gmail inbox and calendar right inside the CRM, while Attio makes you build everything yourself and can feel overwhelming.

Only use Attio if you need to set up custom workflows from scratch; otherwise, Copper is the clear pick for anyone using Google Workspace.

Copper makes CRM automation and workflow setup dead simple for Google Workspace teams. You get built-in email automation, form integration, and a Chrome extension that lets you manage your inbox, tasks, and CRM data without ever leaving Gmail or Calendar. The whole system is designed so non-technical users can adopt it quickly, and the native Zapier and Make connectors mean even custom integrations are straightforward.

Attio is all about flexibility and customization for technical users who want to build their own workflows from scratch. The automation engine is powerful and lets you connect actions across the CRM, but you need to be comfortable with automation tools and structuring data yourself. Small and medium businesses looking for a plug-and-play experience will find the setup process heavy and the learning curve steep.

If you want automation that just works out of the box with minimal fuss, especially if your team lives in Google Workspace, Copper is the clear pick. Attio is only the better choice if you actually want to design every workflow yourself and have the technical chops to pull it off. For most teams, Copper gets you running faster and with less hassle.

### Copper vs Close
**Live Link**: [https://efficient.app/compare/copper-vs-close](https://efficient.app/compare/copper-vs-close)

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
Copper lets you run your entire Gmail inbox and calendar right inside the CRM, while Close forces you into its phone system and falls short on integration.

Only use Close if your team is all about high-volume calling and you need the autodialer more than anything; otherwise, stick with Copper.

Copper absolutely crushes Close when it comes to Google Workspace integration. If your team lives in Gmail and Google Calendar, Copper lets you manage your entire inbox, tasks, and contacts without ever leaving those apps. The Chrome extension means you barely notice you're in a CRM at all. Emails flow in automatically, and every core workflow just happens inside the tools you already use.

Close can sync emails from Google Workspace and Microsoft 365, but it doesn't come close to the immersion or depth Copper offers. The email experience in Close is described as weaker than what you get in Gmail, and you're stuck using their built-in VoIP system, which can feel rigid if you want flexibility or rely on anything besides phone outreach.

If deep, seamless Google Workspace integration is what matters most, Copper is the only real choice here. Close is only strong if your team's entire workflow is high-volume calling and you value the built-in autodialer above all else. For everyone else, Copper makes your CRM feel like a natural extension of Google Workspace, while Close forces you into its own way of working.

### Copper vs GoHighLevel
**Live Link**: [https://efficient.app/compare/copper-vs-highlevel](https://efficient.app/compare/copper-vs-highlevel)

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
Copper gives you deep Google Workspace integration so you can manage Gmail right inside the CRM, which GoHighLevel does not match.

Avoid GoHighLevel and use Copper if you want a CRM that actually works well with Google tools.

Ease of Learning
Ease of Learning
Best
Best

Copper is a no-brainer if you want your team up and running fast. Since it lives inside Gmail and Google Calendar, anyone already using Google Workspace can just start working without having to learn a new system or spend time on training. You add contacts or manage tasks right where you already work, so onboarding is basically instant.

GoHighLevel is the total opposite. You have to spend hours just setting it up before your team can even touch it, and then there's a steep learning curve on top. If your team isn't technical, they'll keep running into issues and need constant help just to keep things moving.

If getting started quickly matters, Copper is way ahead. GoHighLevel will slow you down and frustrate everyone who isn't already an expert.

Workflow Presence
Workflow Presence
Best
Best

Copper makes it easy for your sales team to work where they already spend their time, letting you handle CRM tasks right inside Gmail or Google Calendar. This means way less context switching and almost no need to dig through a separate CRM interface.

GoHighLevel does the opposite. You're forced to use its slow, clunky dashboard for everything, which just adds friction and admin headaches. Expect to waste time troubleshooting and answering team questions instead of actually working in your usual tools.

If you care about reducing context switching and want your CRM to fit seamlessly into your team's daily workflow, Copper is the clear pick. GoHighLevel just gets in the way.

Team Adoption
Team Adoption
Best
Best

Copper is way ahead when it comes to team adoption. It plugs right into Gmail and Google Calendar, so everyone just uses it naturally without having to learn anything new or bounce between tabs. Even people who usually avoid new tools end up actually sticking with Copper because it lives where they already work.

GoHighLevel, on the other hand, is a headache from day one. The interface is clunky, things break all the time, and you'll spend your time fixing problems instead of getting your team to use it. Most people will resist using it, and you'll constantly be dragged into tech support mode just to keep it afloat.

If you care about your team actually using the CRM without endless frustration, Copper is the obvious pick. GoHighLevel just makes everything harder and your team will hate it.

AI Assistance
AI Assistance
Draw

Neither GoHighLevel nor Copper nails AI Assistance, but Copper edges ahead if you actually want to save time with AI in your CRM. GoHighLevel's AI feels slapped on just for show and won't make a dent in your workflow. It talks a big game but doesn't deliver anything truly helpful.

Copper sticks to the basics with lighter AI features, like writing AI emails. Its new Copper GPT is separate from the main app, so you won't get a true in-app assistant, but at least the AI features it does have work as described.

If you care about genuinely useful AI that helps with real tasks, Copper is the less disappointing pick. GoHighLevel's AI just isn't worth your attention.

Integrations
Integrations
Best
Best

Copper blows GoHighLevel out of the water when it comes to integrations. Copper's sync with Google Workspace isn't just deep, it's immediate and retroactive, so everything's up to date from the start. Plus, with a flexible API and easy Zapier or webhook connections, you can hook it into basically any tool you need.

GoHighLevel, on the other hand, piles on features but leaves you with clunky, unreliable integrations that barely work. Expect frustration, not seamless workflows. If you care about integrations that actually add value and don't break your flow, Copper is the only real choice here.

### Copper vs HubSpot
**Live Link**: [https://efficient.app/compare/copper-vs-hubspot](https://efficient.app/compare/copper-vs-hubspot)

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
If you're a small-medium sized team, we recommend using Copper over HubSpot (especially if you're just looking for a CRM and don't need HubSpot's marketing automation features). Copper has an infinitely better user experience and is more affordable in the long-run.

Editor's Verdict
Editor's Verdict
Main
Category
Main Category
Copy Link
Close All
Ease of Learning
Ease of Learning
Best
Best

Copper is massively easier to pick up because it works right inside Gmail and Google Calendar, so anyone already using Google Workspace can just start using it with no real learning curve. There's no new interface to figure out and no bouncing between tabs, so teams can actually be up and running the same day without training headaches.

HubSpot, on the other hand, makes you jump through setup hoops just to get basics like email logging to work, and even small technical missteps can block you from getting started. Instead of diving in, you'll be slowed down by setup quirks and troubleshooting, which kills any hope of same-day onboarding.

If you want your team using the CRM today without confusion or technical roadblocks, Copper is the clear pick. HubSpot just isn't built for instant adoption.

Workflow Presence
Workflow Presence
Best
Best

Copper keeps your sales team in the zone by letting you handle tasks, add contacts, and track activity right inside Gmail or Google Calendar, so you barely interact with the CRM interface at all. That means almost zero context switching and no admin headaches.

HubSpot, on the other hand, constantly interrupts your workflow. If you don't check every box or connect every email exactly right, your conversations can vanish from the CRM and you're left manually tracking things down. This extra friction breaks your flow and slows everything down.

If you want your CRM to just blend into your daily tools and not make you babysit email threads, Copper is the clear pick here. HubSpot makes you work for it, while Copper just works where you already are.

Team Adoption
Team Adoption
Best
Best

Copper makes it almost impossible for your team to ignore the CRM, since it lives right inside Gmail and Google Calendar. No extra tabs or clunky switching means even the least techy teammate actually sticks with it. That direct Google Workspace integration wipes out the usual adoption headaches.

There's no info about how easy it is to get your team using HubSpot, so you're left guessing. If you want your whole crew to actually use the CRM without pushback or drop-off, Copper is the clear choice.

AI Assistance
AI Assistance
Draw

Copper and HubSpot both fall short on genuinely useful AI features that save you time, but for different reasons.

Copper barely brings AI into the workflow. You can use it to write AI emails, but anything deeper just isn't there. Their new Copper GPT is a totally separate ChatGPT app, not a built-in assistant, so it doesn't actually help in your daily CRM tasks.

HubSpot claims to log emails automatically, but it's finicky. If you don't check every box, right client, extension, contact, address, emails just won't log, and you won't know until it's too late. Instead of saving time, you end up babysitting the process, which defeats the point of AI assistance.

If you're picking purely for AI features that actually help, neither really nails it, but Copper's limitations are about missing features, while HubSpot's are about features that should work but often don't. Given the choice, I'd go with Copper, since at least you know what you're getting and won't get caught off guard by missing data. But honestly, neither is a strong pick if real AI help is what you need.

Integrations
Integrations
Draw

Copper stands out for integrations, especially if you're using Google Workspace. It pulls in a full year of emails instantly and keeps everything synced without you needing to fiddle with settings or worry about missing data. The API is flexible, so connecting with other tools through Zapier or webhooks is straightforward.

HubSpot makes you work for it. Getting emails to log automatically is a hassle and can break if you miss a step. You have to manage extensions, check boxes, and hope it all lines up, or you risk losing key conversations. It's a constant chore, especially compared to Copper, which just handles it for you.

If seamless, reliable integrations matter and you don't want to babysit your CRM, Copper is the clear pick. HubSpot only makes sense if you're willing to put in extra effort and don't mind things getting messy.

THE Complete Software Stack Every Small Business Needs 🤫
Watch
92.9K views
Editor's Verdict
Editor's Verdict
Copy Link
Close All
Ease of Learning
Ease of Learning
Best
Best

Copper is way smoother for teams already using Google Workspace, since it lives right inside Gmail and Google Calendar. There's basically no new interface to learn, so people can just start using it right away without extra training or hassle.

Pipedrive is also quick to pick up, especially with its simple visual pipeline, and most reps can figure out the basics in minutes. But the constant upgrade prompts and add-ons clutter things up, so new users end up distracted and onboarding isn't as clean.

If you want your team up and running the same day with zero confusion, Copper is the clear pick because it actually keeps things simple and gets out of your way. Pipedrive is close, but the interface clutter makes it less seamless for new teams.

Workflow Presence
Workflow Presence
Best
Best

Copper makes it way easier to handle sales work without leaving Gmail or Google Calendar. You can add contacts, track activity, and follow up with leads from right inside the apps your team already uses, so there's barely any jumping between tools or extra admin.

Pipedrive keeps all deal info and communication in one place, which helps your team see the full picture without hunting around. The mobile app is handy for updates on the go, but real workflow automation only comes with pricier plans, so day-to-day tasks aren't as smooth unless you pay extra.

If you want your sales team to avoid context switching and just get things done where they already work, Copper is the clear pick. Pipedrive is organized and mobile-friendly, but Copper fits more naturally into your team's flow.

Team Adoption
Team Adoption
Best
Best

Copper just makes team adoption easy if you're already deep in Google Workspace. You never have to leave Gmail or Calendar, so even the least techy person on your team ends up actually using it. There's no separate interface to juggle, no onboarding wall, and nothing to forget, Copper is just always there, in the flow of your day.

Pipedrive feels more like a classic CRM: it's fast to pick up after some training, and the visual pipeline helps sales reps keep things organized. But the interface gets cluttered with upgrade nags and pop-ups, which annoys people enough that some stop logging in regularly. Plus, email sync is limited and manual if you want full history, making it harder to get that "everything just works" feeling. Teams using Microsoft 365 might stick with it, but Google Workspace users will run into friction.

If you want your whole team to actually keep using the CRM without constant reminders or frustration, Copper is the clear pick, especially if you're already living in Gmail and Google Calendar. Pipedrive can work, but you'll have to fight more resistance and clutter to keep everyone engaged.

AI Assistance
AI Assistance
Draw

Pipedrive squeaks ahead on AI Assistance, but only by a bit. Both Copper and Pipedrive have pretty basic AI features that don't really save you much time, but Pipedrive at least offers some extra touches like enriching contact data and giving surface-level deal suggestions, along with the usual AI email help. These are still pretty generic and won't impress anyone looking for serious automation, but they're more integrated than what Copper provides.

Copper's AI is lighter and feels tacked on. The new Copper GPT is actually a separate ChatGPT app, not a built-in assistant, so you're not getting useful AI right where you need it. If you want anything beyond simple AI email writing, you'll be disappointed.

If AI features matter even a little, Pipedrive is the slightly better bet, but honestly, neither is going to wow you here. Pick Pipedrive if you need to choose, but don't expect much from either one.

Integrations
Integrations
Draw

Copper stands out for its deep, seamless integration with Google Workspace, instantly syncing a full year of emails so your CRM is up to date from day one. This level of automatic, valuable connection goes beyond just linking tools together.

Pipedrive also has a strong, flexible API that makes it easy to connect with most third-party tools and works well as a central hub. But it doesn't match Copper's out-of-the-box depth, especially for Google Workspace users.

If you care about integrations that actually add value and want things to just work, especially if you're in Google Workspace, Copper is the clear pick. Pipedrive is solid, but Copper's integrations are simply on another level.

Why Businesses Are Moving Away From Salesforce (Salesforce vs Copper)

Why Businesses Are Moving Away From Salesforce (Salesforce vs Copper)
Watch
1.6K views
Editor's Verdict
Editor's Verdict
Copy Link
Close All
Ease of Learning
Ease of Learning
Best
Best

Copper is way easier to pick up because it works right inside Gmail and Google Calendar, so if your team already uses Google Workspace, there's barely anything new to learn. You can get started the same day, no training headaches or extra setup.

Salesforce is the exact opposite here. You're looking at weeks or even months before your team is up and running, unless you have everything mapped out and a big consulting budget. It's just not built for quick adoption.

If you want your team using a CRM today without a huge learning curve, Copper is the obvious choice.

Workflow Presence
Workflow Presence
Best
Best

Copper keeps your sales team in their groove by letting you handle CRM tasks right inside Gmail or Google Calendar. You barely touch a separate CRM interface, so there's almost no context switching. Automated follow-ups send straight from your own Gmail, making it feel like a natural extension of your daily workflow.

Salesforce, on the other hand, demands you bend your entire process around it. Unless you're ready to invest heavily and bring in outside help, it creates more friction and forces your team to adapt to its way of working. Even with integrations, every interaction feels like a chore, and it doesn't blend into your team's habits.

If you want your CRM to show up where your team already works and actually reduce admin headaches, Copper is the clear pick here. Salesforce only makes sense if you're a huge team willing to rework everything just to fit it in. For everyone else, Copper just fits.

Team Adoption
Team Adoption
Best
Best

Copper makes it ridiculously easy for your team to actually use the CRM since it lives right inside Gmail and Google Calendar. No extra tabs, no onboarding headaches, and even the least techy person on your team ends up using it because it's just part of their normal workflow.

Salesforce, on the other hand, is a slog for most teams unless you're already running super complex processes and have the cash to throw at consultants. For startups or anyone who wants something simple, your team is way more likely to avoid it, and you'll end up nagging people to use it instead of just getting value.

If you care about your team actually adopting the CRM and sticking with it, Copper is the obvious pick. Salesforce just creates more friction and team pushback unless you're in that tiny slice of companies that need all the complexity.

AI Assistance
AI Assistance
Draw

If you need AI features that actually save time and you run a big, complex business with the budget for expert consultants, Salesforce is built for you. Its AI tools deliver real value in those demanding setups and genuinely help streamline work, but they're overkill for anyone with simpler needs.

Copper barely offers more than basic AI email writing, and its new GPT tool sits outside the main app instead of acting as a real assistant. For most users, Copper's AI is just surface level and won't move the needle.

So if you're a large, process-heavy team with resources to set things up, Salesforce is the clear pick for useful AI. But if you don't fit that profile, neither CRM will give you much day-to-day AI help, Salesforce just happens to be the only one that's truly effective for its target audience.

Integrations
Integrations
Draw

Copper nails integrations for anyone deep in the Google Workspace world. Out of the box, it instantly backfills a year of your emails and keeps everything tightly synced, so you're up and running with real data right away. Plus, their flexible API and support for Zapier or webhooks means you can connect to just about anything without hassle.

Salesforce can deliver serious integration depth, but only if you're an enterprise with the budget and patience for consultants to set it all up. The payoff is rock-solid, valuable connections, but there's no quick start, it's a heavy lift and not for teams wanting immediate results or simple plug-ins.

If you want integrations that just work and add value from day one, Copper is the clear choice. Salesforce only makes sense if you're ready to invest big and need custom, enterprise-grade setups. For most teams, Copper gets you there faster and with less pain.

### Copper vs Streak
**Live Link**: [https://efficient.app/compare/copper-vs-streak](https://efficient.app/compare/copper-vs-streak)

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
Streak CRM is built into Gmail and is super convenient, but when we tried it we found it frustrating over time—it slows down Gmail, data can get unstructured and messy, which results in bad CRM habits. It's more like a glorified spreadsheet than a true CRM.

Copper CRM, on the other hand, is structured for scale, and builds a better foundation for businesses.

1
Copper
Best
Best

Best CRM for Google Workspace

Best CRM for Google Workspace
Claim deals
2
Streak

For individuals looking for Google Sheets but CRM

For individuals looking for Google Sheets but CRM
Read review
Editor's Verdict
Editor's Verdict
Copy Link

We often get the question "How does Copper compare to Streak?" With Copper's basic pricing starting at $25 per user per month, and Streak starting at $0 per user per month, it's a good question. We've answered this question in private more times than we can count, so last time we got asked we decided to post it here as a blog post. Keep reading for a candid comparison of Streak VS. Copper written by Efficient App's Founder, Alex Bass.

Super curious, any chance you're familiar with Streak? Streak seems to similarly tightly integrate with Google and I could get a discount on Streak too. Do you have any knowledge of Streak or red flags vs Copper (or major advantages with Copper)? I can't find much literature online comparing the two.

I'm actually intimately familiar with Streak CRM, that's actually the first CRM I cut my teeth on. I implemented it into my own business in 2014, and then my stepfather's business in 2015, and then recommended it to 43North as a CRM in 2016 (of which they used it for a year as per my recommendation). I really liked it, until I didn't.

My Journey With Streak

Over time, I had a lot of frustrations with Streak, it was more of spreadsheet than a CRM, and the killer was how much it slowed down Gmail. Gmail would take 2-4 seconds to load normally, and then streak would take 20-30 seconds to load on top of that. So when I wanted to quickly do something even in Gmail, it was being bogged down by Streak.

I started realizing that while I thought I liked the idea of an all-in-one tool (baked right into Gmail), it was actually the thing that made me despise it.

The Functionality of Streak

Streak positions itself as a CRM, but it doesn't function in many ways as a normal CRM does. What Streak really is, is a glorified spreadsheet within Gmail.

And while many might be like "what's wrong with that?" it's that it will lead to bad data entry practices and duplicate information without a single source of truth.

Which is the core problem with spreadsheets: it's so easy to create a new column of a data-point that you want to "track" on a standalone pipeline, and the next thing you know, sales reps are building out their pipeline structure, storing information on the "Box" (equivalent to an Opportunity in a traditional CRM), that should be stored on the person/company record traditionally.

Problems Mounting Up With Streak
Duplicate data

What I saw after these 2-3 years of using Streak, was that my clients and I all had tons of duplicate data across different pipelines, wondering which one is the most up-to-date data.

Duplicate records of "John Doe" with the same and different email addresses, because "John Doe" was in 6 different boxes (separate processes), some for cold outreach, some used by other divisions of the company, and complete separation of data.

Email issues

Email delivery issues arose because they were doing cold outreach with their main email domain because it was convenient (when they shouldn't as it hurts domain deliverability) and because Streak made adding data easy - like a spreadsheet.

But a CRM shouldn't be that easy to add whatever data-points you'd like. Part of a good CRM is keeping it clean and treating it as your company's single-source-of-truth.

Clean data comes from having thoughtfulness around what data-points you're adding and to what entities (Leads/People/Opportunities/Companies). Not quickly creating columns in a spreadsheet to do a quick one-off mail-merge email blast.

Streak is convenient, it has some cool features, but they come at a cost. Streak is a CRM that you'll have to move off of with scale, and when you do, a CRM consultant like us will steer clear, because there's no consistency in data. There's not even consistency in entity naming schemes (they have their own proprietary "box" terminology).

Start with a ‘real’ CRM

So moving to a properly structured CRM like Copper/Salesforce/Hubspot/Pipedrive will result in either throwing away much of your data or hiring a data cleanser to take a ton of junk data and attempting to merge it all and fix it all.

With 43North, we spent at least 40 hours cleansing their data to move them over to Copper. Streak was a huge mistake and I had majorly regretted recommending it back in the day to them.

Their team also had slight frustration because things like mail merge weren't as "simple" with Copper, so they got used to some of the convenience, though at a major cost. John in ABC department could move so quickly doing things, but at the expense of having a complete mess of data disconnected from the rest of the team. Is it worth it? Well, you tell me — you're not building company IP when you do that, you're trying to do things quickly with duct tape.

All that being said, when you get to tens of thousands of records, your Gmail is going to be loading so slowly which leads to other frustrations. It's a good temporary solution for a small team that is okay with moving quick and breaking things, but you'll have to move off of it in the future with scale, and when you do, companies like us will recommend starting from scratch as that is easier than it will be to fix what already exists.

But I have a free/cheap discount deal with Streak!

This question gets asked to us so often. Especially when it comes to pricing ("if I can get a free/discounted CRM, shouldn't I just go with that?") - and when you start looking into CRM's built for Google, you're going to stumble upon Streak.

I'd recommend using any traditional CRM (literally Pipedrive/Hubspot) over using Streak, because what Streak does is teaches your team bad habits and gets used to having convenient features, that won't scale.

So when you inevitably move to a proper CRM, because it's needed with scale, your team is going to be incredibly frustrated, but your company will need to do it. So it's a short-term solution that does cool things, but it's not what I would recommend if you're looking to invest in good software and build IP and a single-source-of-truth + processes for your company.

Copper CRM For The Win  

Copper is trying to build a more user-friendly and enjoyable CRM, one that can be used instead of Salesforce for the SMB market. Streak is trying to bridge the gap between spreadsheets and CRM. They aren't a great spreadsheet tool and they aren't a great CRM. Their competitor is Google Sheets/Excel. While Copper's is too, they are also trying to compete with proper CRMs, enabling you to build a better company foundation. Use this link to try Copper for free.

Hope you enjoyed this candid comparison of Streak VS. Copper CRM. We typically work with businesses that are going $1.5+ mil in revenue/are ready to invest in building their CRM infrastructure for scale. Get in touch to learn how we can help you implement your CRM the right way from the start.

### Copper vs folk
**Live Link**: [https://efficient.app/compare/copper-vs-folk](https://efficient.app/compare/copper-vs-folk)

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
We love Copper & folk! We recommend folk for teams who are new to using a CRM (perhaps have never used one before and want something super simple and quick to setup).

We recommend Copper for teams with more established processes who are looking to automate their processes and integrate their CRM with other third party software.

THE Complete Software Stack Every Small Business Needs 🤫

THE Complete Software Stack Every Small Business Needs 🤫
Watch
Editor's Verdict
Editor's Verdict
Main
Category
Main Category
Copy Link
Close All
Ease of Learning
Ease of Learning
Draw

Both folk and Copper make onboarding fast, but the way they do it is different. folk feels instantly familiar if you've ever used spreadsheets, so setup is simple and you won't waste time figuring out the basics. Pipeline templates and one-click contact capture mean you can get started right away.

Copper is also quick to onboard but is a bit more traditional as a CRM and they get you to focus on your processes a bit more in order to set it up properly (e.g. do you want leads enabled or not?). That said, Copper is the more scalable solution for businesses that plan to use a CRM for years to come, or that have used HubSpot or Salesforce in other businesses so the additional time investment is worth it.

Use folk if you're dipping your toes into the world of CRM, but Copper if you're planning on setting it up for the long-haul.

Workflow Presence
Workflow Presence
Draw

Both Copper and folk keeps your sales team in flow compared to other CRMs. Their Chrome extensions are both helpful for adding contacts and managing relationships right from LinkedIn & Gmail.

You can handle tasks, add contacts, and track activity without breaking your flow, which cuts down on context switching. Automated follow-ups also send directly from your Gmail, making your outreach feel personal and not like a mass blast.

Team Adoption
Team Adoption
Draw

CRM adoption is typically tough in general as sales teams like to move fast, and entering notes and data slows them down. That said, Copper and folk are two of the easiest tools to onboard your team on.

The reason is because they both have a Chrome extension that opens right within Gmail, so for less tech-savvy team members, you can just teach them how to use that to start.

Then, once they get into the habit of using the Chrome extension, the second layer of training can show them the bigger picture with the CRM dashboard.

Ultimately, if you set up automated email follow-ups and sales reps start feeling like the CRM is helping them close more deals, they'll want to use it more and more, making adoption natural.

AI Assistance
AI Assistance
Best
Best

folk actually takes work off your plate with its AI. You get Magic Fields that handle personalized group emails and clean up messy contact data in one go, so you skip the usual manual slog of fixing info or writing repetitive outreach.

Copper barely scratches the surface here. You can write AI emails, but anything more advanced is missing. Their new Copper GPT is just a separate chatbot for questions, not a real workflow assistant inside the CRM.

If you want AI that saves you real time in your CRM routine, folk is the clear choice. Copper just doesn't deliver meaningful AI help beyond the basics.

Integrations
Integrations
Best
Best

Copper is much better with integrations. Copper's Google Workspace sync is deeper and more automatic, pulling in a full year of emails right away and even syncs over your Google Drive files to relevant contacts and opportunities. If you rely on Google tools, there's nothing better. Plus, Copper's API is wide open and flexible, so you can hook it into pretty much anything, whether that's through Zapier or direct webhooks.

folk also pulls in historical emails from Gmail, but the moment you need more than basic integrations with third-party tools, you'll hit walls. folk's API is limited, and even Zapier can't smooth over all the gaps, especially with certain field types.

If you just need simple imports and light syncing, folk is fine for solo users or tiny teams starting out. But for anyone who actually cares about seamless, valuable integrations, especially if you want to scale or work with a real team, Copper is the top pick.

### Customer.io vs Mailchimp
**Live Link**: [https://efficient.app/compare/customer-vs-mailchimp](https://efficient.app/compare/customer-vs-mailchimp)

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
Customer.io lets you build segmented onboarding flows and manage communications visually, while Mailchimp buries you in confusing options and pushes templates that hurt engagement.

Only use Customer.io if you need visual flows and Slack integration, otherwise skip Mailchimp and look for a simpler email tool.

Editor's Verdict

If you care about actually getting effective email marketing done without headaches, neither Customer.io nor Mailchimp is the answer here.

Mailchimp makes even basic email sending feel like a chore by drowning you in options and pushing fancy templates that end up hurting engagement. Setting up a simple campaign turns into a confusing maze, and the focus on heavily branded emails misses the mark now that people want messages that feel personal.

Customer.io isn't built for traditional email marketing at all. It's great for managing onboarding flows and automating communication inside your product, but if you're looking for a tool to handle straightforward marketing emails, it just doesn't fit.

Bottom line: if you need a real email marketing tool, skip both. Mailchimp will frustrate you with complexity and ineffective templates, and Customer.io just isn't meant for this kind of job. Look elsewhere for something simpler and actually focused on email marketing.

### Customer.io vs Reply
**Live Link**: [https://efficient.app/compare/customer-vs-reply](https://efficient.app/compare/customer-vs-reply)

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
Customer.io gives you clear control over segmented onboarding and customer communications with smooth Slack integration, while Reply's unreliable CRM syncing and shallow integrations force you into constant workarounds.

Only use Reply if you never need to sync with a CRM or care about pricing surprises; otherwise, Customer.io is the safer pick.

Editor's Verdict
Editor's Verdict
Copy Link

Customer.io nails integrations and makes it painless to connect with tools like Slack, push updates, and handle segmented onboarding flows visually. Its native integrations are flexible and actually work, so you can count on it to keep your workflows smooth if you need to sync events or updates across platforms.

Reply, on the other hand, constantly lets you down if you care about real CRM integration. Its syncing is unreliable, integrations are shallow, and getting your data out is frustrating. Instead of connecting your tools, you end up patching together messy workarounds, and you never feel like your systems are truly talking to each other.

If seamless, reliable integrations matter at all, go with Customer.io. Reply just isn't built for anyone who depends on their CRM or wants clean data flow.

### Deel vs QuickBooks Online
**Live Link**: [https://efficient.app/compare/deel-vs-quickbooks](https://efficient.app/compare/deel-vs-quickbooks)

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
Deel handles global payroll, contracts, and compliance smoothly in over 150 countries, while QuickBooks Online only really fits US accounting and struggles with payroll or HR.

Only use QuickBooks Online if you need US-focused accounting; use Deel for anything involving international hiring or payroll.

1

Editor's Verdict
Editor's Verdict

Deel completely outclasses QuickBooks Online when it comes to handling global payroll, HR, and compliance. Deel is built from the ground up for international hiring, letting you pay employees and contractors in over 150 countries, auto-generating invoices, and keeping you fully compliant with local labor laws and taxes. It also takes care of onboarding, contracts, and even helps with things like background checks and equipment for new hires. If your team is hiring or managing people across borders, Deel just handles everything without the usual headaches.

QuickBooks Online, on the other hand, only really covers US payroll as an afterthought. The payroll and HR features in QBO are clunky, limited, and described as something you'll want to ditch as soon as you can. It's not designed for international compliance or payments, and even for US payroll, the experience is frustrating and time-consuming.

So if you need serious payroll and HR, especially across multiple countries, Deel is the obvious pick. Only stick with QBO if you're a US-only business and even then, it's not the tool you want for payroll or HR.

### Deel vs Rippling
**Live Link**: [https://efficient.app/compare/deel-vs-rippling](https://efficient.app/compare/deel-vs-rippling)

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
Deel streamlines hiring, payroll, and HR across 150 countries, making global workforce management easy, while Rippling focuses on large enterprises wanting payroll, HR, IT, finance, and spend tools in one place.

Only use Rippling if you need its all-in-one approach for a big company; choose Deel if you want hassle-free global hiring and compliance.

Deel stands out for handling global hiring, payroll, and compliance across 150+ countries, making it the clear pick if you need to manage international teams or pay people in different currencies. It's built to simplify the mess of contracts, local labor laws, and taxes, and actually keeps up with country-by-country changes. This makes it a lifesaver for HR teams dealing with the headaches of hiring and paying people around the world.

Rippling, on the other hand, tries to be an all-in-one suite for HR, payroll, IT, and finance, but its strength is aimed at large enterprises wanting everything under one roof. For anyone focused on international hiring or payroll, Rippling feels like overkill and doesn't specifically address the global complexity Deel is designed to solve.

If you care about streamlining international workforce management, Deel is the obvious choice. Rippling only makes sense if you're a big company that wants to jam HR, IT, and finance together and aren't prioritizing global payroll or compliance.

Dia Browser takes everything that made Arc's sidebar and tab management great, then layers in a modern AI chat experience right at the core. The chat sidebar isn't just a bolt-on, it's deeply integrated, fast, and comes with pre-built "Skills" that let you interact with web content directly. If you care about having an AI co-pilot guiding your browsing and want the most refined version of the Arc foundation, Dia is the clear pick.

Arc still nails productivity for people who obsess over organization, keyboard shortcuts, and separating work from personal. If your priority is pure productivity features and you don't care about AI, Arc feels more focused and stable, especially if you're on MacOS. But Dia is catching up fast and already matches Arc's best features, with the bonus of a built-in, well-designed AI chat.

If you want the future of browsing with AI at the center, go with Dia. If you're a hardcore productivity user who doesn't need AI and is on MacOS, Arc still delivers. For anyone open to AI in their workflow, Dia is now the more compelling option.

### Dia Browser vs Brave
**Live Link**: [https://efficient.app/compare/dia-vs-brave](https://efficient.app/compare/dia-vs-brave)

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
Dia Browser gives you the slick features from Arc plus an AI chatbot built in, while Brave is all about strict privacy, ad blocking, and crypto tools that can feel like work to manage.

Only use Brave if you really care about privacy and crypto and are willing to deal with seed phrases and data risks; otherwise, pick Dia Browser.

If you care most about privacy and want features like built-in ad blocking, tracking protection, and a crypto wallet with rewards, Brave is the clear pick. It's designed from the ground up for people obsessed with privacy, even if that means some hassle managing seed phrases or risking lost data if you forget your key. For this crowd, Brave's approach to privacy is unmatched and core to the experience.

Dia Browser, on the other hand, doesn't focus on privacy as its main selling point. It's all about AI features, a refined interface, and powerful browsing tools inspired by Arc. Privacy isn't called out as a standout benefit here, and the review doesn't mention anything beyond standard protections you'd find in most modern browsers.

So if your top priority is privacy to the point of sacrificing convenience, Brave is built for you. If you just want a solid browser with smart AI features and aren't hardcore about privacy, Dia is a better fit. For privacy-first users, Brave takes it. For everyone else, look to Dia.

Dia Browser gives you all the standout features from Arc plus a built-in AI chatbot, while Chromium just offers the basic foundation without those perks.

If you want a browser to actually use and enjoy, go with Dia. Chromium is only for people who want to mess with the underlying tech, not for anyone looking to just get things done.

### Dia Browser vs Comet Browser
**Live Link**: [https://efficient.app/compare/dia-vs-comet](https://efficient.app/compare/dia-vs-comet)

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
Dia has a significantly better tab management experience compared Comet, so it's easier to actually organize and get through work.

But when it comes to sheer AI browser functionality, Comet has the full power of Perplexity (free agent mode), while Dia more relies just on AI chat in the sidebar and is a wrapper for OpenAI and others.

Best AI Browser? Comet vs ChatGPT Atlas vs Dia (Honest Review)

Best AI Browser? Comet vs ChatGPT Atlas vs Dia (Honest Review)

Recommended Alternatives

1
ChatGPT Atlas
Best
Best

Best AI Browser for ChatGPT users

Best AI Browser for ChatGPT users
Get 100% off
Editor's Verdict
Editor's Verdict
Main Category
Main Category
Copy Link
Potential and Capabilities

While Comet is promising with Perplexity at the core, integration with email and calendar, plus the ability to actually take over and click around for you, Dia is what I am opening and using every day. It's tab management is just significantly better.

Browser Interaction

Comet can interact with open tabs, clean up and group tabs, similarly to Dia. That said, Comet allows you to actually talk with your browser via a voice assistant, and even navigate the pages your on at times, showing you a deeper layer of browser interaction that you don't get with Dia.

History & Memory

Right now Dia allows you to reference your history with @history, allowing it to go a bit deeper on personalization based on your prior searches and pages visited. Comet doesn't go too deep into history or memory as of now, bup Perplexity has said that they are diving more into memory in the future (which will make its way into Comet).

Dia Skills

Dia has skills that you can trigger with /skillname which are effectively pre-set prompting before a search is sent or chat is completed. They also have a marketplace where you can grab these skills (prompts) and modify them for your own usage. It's definitely a standout feature.

Monetization and Business Model

Perplexity knows how to make money and has way more resources, so I believe they'll actually have a sustainable business model (they already do). That means they'll be able to do more and figure out how to monetize the browser effectively.

Search Functionality

Perplexity is a search engine that's already good, and I use it alongside ChatGPT. Dia is mostly using an OpenAI wrapper (and Anthropic) for their search, so I'm just generally less bullish because I find myself missing both Perplexity and ChatGPT when using Dia, they just have less IP as a whole.

Overall Outlook

With all of that said, Perplexity is already a fantastic search engine. So taking that and building a browser around it makes a lot of sense. In-fact, it's getting me even more excited for OpenAI's upcoming browser as they are leading the charge when it comes to chat and search.

I'm unsure if just pure product and design quality can win in this space. Love how Dia looks and the team behind it is exceptional, just not totally sure if it's enough being more of a wrapper of these other AI tools at the core.

### Dia Browser vs Edge
**Live Link**: [https://efficient.app/compare/dia-vs-edge](https://efficient.app/compare/dia-vs-edge)

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
Dia Browser gives you all the best features from Arc like its sidebar and tab management, plus a built in AI chatbot, while Edge just wraps ChatGPT in some extra menus.

Only use Edge if you have no other choice because Dia Browser is simply stronger for AI and daily browsing.

Editor's Verdict
Editor's Verdict
Copy Link

Dia Browser absolutely outclasses Edge when it comes to AI integration. Dia builds its entire experience around a deeply integrated, modern AI chatbot that actually interacts with your browsing, offers refined features like its signature sidebar, and includes unique tools like Skills for running context-aware prompts on any page. The AI isn't just bolted on, it's core to how Dia works.

Edge, on the other hand, just slaps a Copilot sidebar on top, which is basically a ChatGPT wrapper with some pre-set functions. There's no real sense of the browser adapting to you or the web pages you're on. The AI feels tacked on, not baked in, and the rest of the browser still shows its age.

If what you care about is having a browser that genuinely weaves AI into every part of your workflow, Dia is the clear choice. Edge only really makes sense if you're locked into Microsoft 365 and want everything Microsoft, but for anyone who wants a modern, AI-first browsing experience, Dia is miles ahead.

### Dia Browser vs Firefox
**Live Link**: [https://efficient.app/compare/dia-vs-firefox](https://efficient.app/compare/dia-vs-firefox)

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
Dia Browser brings all the standout features of Arc plus a built-in AI chatbot, while Firefox trades off extension support and site compatibility for privacy.

Only use Firefox if privacy is your top concern; otherwise, Dia Browser is the clear pick for most people.

1
Dia Browser

Superhuman Mail

Editor's Verdict
Editor's Verdict
Copy Link

Dia Browser absolutely crushes Firefox when it comes to extension and website compatibility. Dia is built on Chromium, so it supports the massive library of Chrome extensions and renders websites exactly as intended, just like the dominant browsers most developers target. You're not going to run into those annoying moments where a site breaks or some extension you rely on just isn't available.

Firefox's privacy focus is nice, but the reality is you'll hit constant friction because most extensions aren't supported unless someone specifically rebuilds them for Firefox. Even then, you're more likely to see websites not working right since Firefox's tiny market share means many sites aren't tested for it. Most people who use Firefox end up keeping a second browser around for when things inevitably don't work.

If you only want everything to just work, with the full power of extensions and reliable site rendering, Dia is the obvious pick. Firefox is only worth considering if you're dead set on privacy and willing to deal with constant compatibility tradeoffs. For everyone else, Dia is the better experience by a mile.

### Dia Browser vs Google Chrome
**Live Link**: [https://efficient.app/compare/dia-vs-chrome](https://efficient.app/compare/dia-vs-chrome)

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
Dia Browser gives you smarter tab management, a standout sidebar, and built in AI chatbot features that Chrome does not offer.

Only use Google Chrome if you rely on Google services and are not on Mac, otherwise Dia Browser is the clear pick for new features and experience.

Editor's Verdict
Editor's Verdict
Copy Link

Dia Browser absolutely runs circles around Chrome when it comes to tab management and sidebar features. The whole experience is modern, thoughtful, and actually solves the mess of tab overload that Chrome never really figured out. Dia's sidebar is not just an add-on, it's the core of the workflow and miles ahead of Chrome's attempt at tab groups, which ended up buried and optional. If you care about organizing lots of tabs or want a sidebar that genuinely helps you navigate and multitask, Dia is the clear winner.

Chrome is steady and familiar, but that's also the problem here. Its tab management is stuck in the past, and even the features it does roll out are slow, safe, and watered down to avoid upsetting its massive user base. Chrome's bookmarks and tab groups feel like afterthoughts, and the review basically calls out that this is by design, Google needs you to get lost so you'll keep searching.

If you want a browser that makes managing tabs and multitasking feel effortless, pick Dia. Chrome is only worth sticking with if you're deeply tied to Google and don't care about better tab management. For everyone else, especially if you're on MacOS, Dia is the obvious upgrade.

### Dia Browser vs Opera
**Live Link**: [https://efficient.app/compare/dia-vs-opera](https://efficient.app/compare/dia-vs-opera)

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
Dia Browser brings over Arc's standout sidebar, tab management, and profiles, but adds a modern AI chatbot at its core, while Opera mainly copies Arc's onboarding and features but feels unchanged aside from flashy animations.

Only use Opera if you have a specific attachment, otherwise Dia Browser is the clear pick for a smarter, more modern experience.

Editor's Verdict
Editor's Verdict
Copy Link

Dia Browser absolutely outclasses Opera on core browser design and feature quality. Dia brings over the best parts of Arc's sidebar, tab management, and profiles, all refined and enhanced with a well-integrated AI chat experience. Every feature feels intentional and modern, not just bolted on.

Opera, on the other hand, is mostly playing catch-up. Its new features like Workspaces and tab groups are direct copies from Arc, but they miss critical details, like true environment separation. The flashy animations don't make up for the fact that it's still the same old Opera underneath, just with a new coat of paint.

If you care about thoughtful design, performance, and genuinely useful features, Dia is the clear pick. Opera only makes sense if you're already locked in and just want something familiar with a few trendy upgrades, but it's not competing at the same level. Go with Dia.

### Dia Browser vs Safari
**Live Link**: [https://efficient.app/compare/dia-vs-safari](https://efficient.app/compare/dia-vs-safari)

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
Dia Browser brings the standout features of Arc with a modern AI chatbot at its core, while Safari sticks to a familiar but dull experience and misses the advanced features and enjoyable UX Dia offers.

Only use Safari if you care most about stability and syncing across Apple devices and dislike new features; otherwise, Dia Browser is the clear pick.

Editor's Verdict
Editor's Verdict
Copy Link

Dia Browser blows Safari out of the water when it comes to advanced features, enjoyable user experience, and overall browser innovation. Dia brings over everything that made Arc's sidebar, tab management, and profiles so good, then layers in a modern AI chat assistant that actually feels integrated and useful. The whole experience is more refined, engaging, and genuinely makes daily browsing feel fresh and productive, especially if you like the idea of having pre-built prompts interact with your web pages.

Safari, on the other hand, is all about giving you the same safe, stable, and frankly boring experience it always has. It's reliable and syncs well across Apple devices, but there's nothing here that stands out as exciting or even particularly efficient. The UI is bland by design, and outside of battery life and Apple ecosystem perks, it just can't compete with the thoughtful design and feature set Dia brings.

If you want a browser that actually makes you enjoy using the web and gives you powerful tools right in the sidebar, Dia is the clear choice. Safari only makes sense if you're allergic to change and care about battery life above literally everything else. Everyone else should go with Dia.

Dia Browser is in a completely different league when it comes to productivity and overall browser experience. Its sidebar, tab management, and profiles are not just bolted-on features but core strengths that made Arc popular, and now they're even more refined in Dia. The AI chat is seamlessly integrated, and the "Skills" feature lets you interact with page content in ways Shift can't touch.

Shift, on the other hand, basically feels like a thin wrapper on top of Chrome with some account switching and sidebar pinning. It hasn't kept pace with new browsers and feels dated, offering little that you couldn't already get with Chrome plus a few extensions like Workona or Toby.

If you want a browser that actually improves how you work and isn't just a slightly tweaked Chrome, Dia Browser is the clear choice. Shift just doesn't bring enough to the table anymore.

Dia Browser is miles ahead when it comes to modern browser features and design. It carries over everything that made Arc stand out, like its refined sidebar and top-tier tab management, and layers in an AI chat experience that actually works well. The team behind it is focused on pushing browser design forward, with resources to keep innovating.

If you want a browser that feels current and actually helps you work better, Dia is the clear pick. Sidekick just doesn't keep up.

Editor's Verdict
Editor's Verdict

Dia Browser is just flat-out more efficient than SigmaOS. It carries over the best productivity features from Arc Browser, like a refined sidebar and advanced tab management, and layers in a fast, integrated AI chat experience that actually works well. You get all of this for free unless you really need unlimited AI, and even then, most people won't hit the free tier limits.

SigmaOS tries some new layout ideas and leans into productivity, but a lot of its features are basically Arc copies that you have to pay for. It also just doesn't have the resources or polish of Dia, and there's no sign it can keep up on efficiency or speed.

If efficiency is what matters, Dia Browser is the clear pick. It's faster, more thoughtfully designed, and gives you more at no cost, while SigmaOS feels like a less capable, paywalled version of what Dia already nails.

### Dialpad vs Aircall
**Live Link**: [https://efficient.app/compare/dialpad-vs-aircall](https://efficient.app/compare/dialpad-vs-aircall)

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
These are both VoIP solutions that we recommend, and have used in our own business.

The distinction for Dialpad vs Aircall ultimately come down to product vs integration. While Dialpad has integrations and an API, unless you're using one of the big CRMs like Salesforce/HubSpot, it doesn't have the best integration.

Aircall on the other hand has more native integrations with more CRMs, but not as great of a UX.

We personally use Dialpad and have a custom integration with Copper as we value the UX it has over Aircall, but would recommend most just go with Aircall instead.

1
Dialpad
Recommended
Recommended

Best overall VoIP

Best overall VoIP
Read review
2
Aircall
Recommended

Best VoIP with native CRM integrations
Try Aircall
Trending Right Now
Trending Right Now
Copy Link
1
Viktor

Best AI agent

Best AI agent
Get $200 off
Editor's Verdict
Editor's Verdict
Copy Link

If you want native, out-of-the-box integrations that actually work with your CRM or helpdesk right away, Aircall nails it. You just connect your tools and calls and texts get logged automatically, with barely any setup or fuss. For most teams who care about integration working smoothly without building anything custom, Aircall is the clear choice.

Dialpad, on the other hand, is picky about which native integrations it supports, and even those can be buggy or incomplete. You can get everything working with their API and Zapier, but it takes real work to set up and maintain, and you can't always trust the integrations listed on their site. If you're technical and willing to build, you can make Dialpad fit, but for anyone who just wants integrations that work out of the box, it's frustrating.

So if easy, reliable integrations are your top priority and you don't want to mess with custom builds, pick Aircall. Only go with Dialpad if you're happy to invest time and resources into building your own connections or if you need features Aircall doesn't offer. For most people who want integrations that just work, Aircall makes life a lot simpler.

### Dialpad vs Close
**Live Link**: [https://efficient.app/compare/dialpad-vs-close](https://efficient.app/compare/dialpad-vs-close)

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
Dialpad gives you a modern VoIP setup with handy AI features and a mobile app that feels like texting, making it easy for business calls or networking, while Close forces you into its built-in VoIP and pricey autodialer.

Only use Close if your sales team is all about high-volume phone outreach and will pay extra for the autodialer; otherwise, pick Dialpad.

1
Dialpad
Recommended
Recommended

Best overall VoIP

Best overall VoIP
Read review
2
Close

For sales-focused cold outreach via VoIP

For sales-focused cold outreach via VoIP
Read review
Trending Right Now
Trending Right Now
Copy Link
1
Superhuman Mail

Best overall email client

Best overall email client
Get 30 days free
Editor's Verdict
Editor's Verdict
Copy Link

Dialpad stands out with a modern, stable VoIP experience that actually feels like texting on your phone, plus strong AI features like sentiment analysis and call review. Its mobile app is more polished and reliable, making everyday business calls and texts smoother. The downside is its native integrations are weak and sometimes buggy, especially with CRMs, so you'll have to invest in custom setups if you want seamless workflow logging.

Close, on the other hand, is built for sales teams that live on the phone and need high-volume outbound calling. Its built-in autodialer is the main draw, letting reps power through call lists efficiently. But you're locked into their VoIP, there's less flexibility, and the mobile app isn't as strong. If you're not running a call-heavy sales operation, you'll feel boxed in fast.

If you want a business phone system that feels natural, works reliably, and offers slick AI tools for general business use, Dialpad is the clear pick. If your sales team lives and dies by high-volume outbound calls and cares most about a built-in autodialer, Close is the specialist, but only for that narrow use case. For everyone else, Dialpad delivers a better, more flexible VoIP experience.

### Dialpad vs JustCall
**Live Link**: [https://efficient.app/compare/dialpad-vs-justcall](https://efficient.app/compare/dialpad-vs-justcall)

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
Dialpad gives you a modern business phone experience with reliable features and a mobile app that feels like SMS, while JustCall has annoying issues with CRM integrations and logging.

Only use JustCall if you just want a cheap, simple calling tool and do not care about reliable CRM workflows; otherwise, pick Dialpad.

Editor's Verdict
Editor's Verdict

Dialpad nails the core VoIP experience with a modern, stable mobile app that actually feels like SMS, plus it offers strong AI features like sentiment analysis and call review that JustCall doesn't touch. If you want your calls and texts to reliably log into your CRM and are willing to put in the work to set up a proper integration, Dialpad gets the job done and feels current.

JustCall, on the other hand, stumbles where it matters most for sales and support teams who care about CRM workflow. Its click-to-dial isn't truly direct from the CRM, and both call and SMS logging are hit-or-miss, forcing you to double-check or work around sync failures. It's fine as a cheap, basic calling tool if your needs are light and you don't mind some friction, but it can't deliver a seamless experience for anyone serious about CRM integration.

If you actually need smooth CRM workflows, reliable logging, and a VoIP app that feels modern, Dialpad is the clear pick. Only consider JustCall if you're on a tight budget and don't mind inconsistent integrations.

Dialpad gives you a modern VoIP experience with built-in AI and a mobile app that feels like SMS, unlike RingCentral which feels outdated and is hard to use.

Only use RingCentral if you are a huge enterprise that cares more about stability than usability or innovation; otherwise, stick with Dialpad.

### Dub Links vs Bitly
**Live Link**: [https://efficient.app/compare/dub-links-vs-bitly](https://efficient.app/compare/dub-links-vs-bitly)

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
Dub Links gives you real-time tracking, reliable analytics, and a modern interface, while Bitly feels outdated and limits key features.

Only use Bitly if you have a specific reason, since Dub Links is just much easier and more useful day to day.

Editor's Verdict
Editor's Verdict
Copy Link

Dub Links completely outclasses Bitly when it comes to analytics and reporting. Dub's dashboard is minimal, clear, and gives you real-time link tracking with deep conversion data that's actually useful for marketers. You get features like QR codes, tags, password protection, expiring links, and geo-targeting all laid out in a way that makes sense.

Bitly's analytics are stuck in the past. The dashboard is clunky, pulling reports is a pain, and you don't get real-time updates. Important features like custom domains and meaningful tracking history are locked behind expensive paywalls, and if you're not paying, Bitly even runs ads against your links, which just looks bad.

If you care at all about reliable, easy-to-read analytics and reporting, Dub Links is the only real choice here. Bitly just doesn't keep up.

Accurate Tracking
Accurate Tracking
Best
Best

Dub Partners blows Cello out of the water on accurate tracking. With Dub Partners, you get airtight attribution thanks to first-party cookies and everything tied back to a single link, so you always know exactly which campaigns and content drive your revenue. It even works smoothly across browsers like Safari and on iPhones, and you never have to stress about ad blockers or tracking gaps.

Cello is missing all of this. You only see basic referral and earnings data, with no way to drill down or verify individual clicks or conversions. If you care about trusting your numbers and actually knowing what's working, Cello leaves you in the dark.

If you want real confidence in your tracking, Dub Partners is the only real choice here.

Payments & Tax Compliance
Payments & Tax Compliance
Best
Best

Dub Partners completely outclasses Cello on payments and tax compliance. With Dub Partners, everything from W-9/W-8 collection to 1099-NEC generation and global payouts is handled in one place, so you and your affiliates aren't stuck chasing paperwork or juggling fragmented payments. Affiliates get a single dashboard and one-click payouts, making admin headaches basically disappear.

Cello automates tax forms and payouts too, but only through PayPal or Venmo, which feels amateur and is a dealbreaker for seasoned affiliates. There's no unified dashboard, so affiliates end up drowning in scattered payments across programs, turning what should be easy into a mess.

If you want payments and tax compliance to just work without constant manual hassle, Dub Partners is the obvious pick. Cello only makes sense if you're fine with a barebones, peer-to-peer setup and don't care about a professional experience.

Partner Experience
Partner Experience
Best
Best

Dub Partners absolutely crushes Cello on partner experience. Dub gives you a single login for all your affiliate programs, plus a clean dashboard that keeps everything organized and easy to access. It's built for publishers, creators, and influencers who need to manage multiple partnerships without the headache.

Cello, on the other hand, makes you juggle a separate account for every program. There's no unified view, so you end up forgetting about programs and wasting time dealing with scattered logins and payments. For anyone serious about affiliate marketing, Cello just feels clunky and easy to neglect.

If you care about a smooth, unified partner experience, Dub Partners is the only real choice here.

Deep Analytics
Deep Analytics
Best
Best

Dub Partners completely outclasses Cello when it comes to deep analytics. Dub's whole system is built around granular, actionable tracking, showing exactly which platforms and individual pieces of content are converting. You get full visibility with real numbers like EPC, LTV, and ROAS right in the dashboard, so you know what's working and can actually improve your results.

Cello barely gives you the basics. You can't see what content or links are converting, so you're left guessing and flying blind. There's no way to double down on what works or get meaningful feedback.

If deep analytics matter at all to you as an affiliate, Dub Partners is the only real choice here. Cello just isn't built for serious tracking or growth.

User Experience
User Experience
Best
Best

Dub Partners is in a completely different league for user experience. You get instant access to all your key analytics, everything is laid out clearly, and you can actually use the data to make decisions without any hassle. Nothing is hidden or buried, so even comparing conversion rates between different content types is effortless.

Cello, on the other hand, only works smoothly if you're a SaaS company running basic peer-to-peer referrals inside your own app. If you're an actual affiliate or publisher, the experience is a mess. You have to manage separate logins for every program, dashboards are confusing, reporting barely tells you anything useful, and payouts feel scattered and unprofessional. It's just not built for affiliates and makes daily use frustrating.

If you want an affiliate platform that's actually enjoyable and efficient to use every day, Dub Partners is the obvious pick. Cello is only passable for internal referrals but falls apart for real affiliate marketing.
