Editor's Verdict
Editor's Verdict
Copy Link
Close All

With both Motion and Asana, you're going to get core project management features like kanban boards and team project/task collaboration (unlike that of Motion vs Reclaim).

You also get an API (and even Zapier for your Asana integration and Motion integration needs). Asana will give you more native out-of-the box integrations with other tools though.

When using both Motion and Asana on-the-go, you're all set with fully featured mobile apps for both (iOS & Android).

So when looking at the key features of both tools, the foundation is there.

The differences between Asana and Motion lie within the teams the tools were created for. Asana is very much for large teams to care about features like project management reporting, dashboards, timeline views, progress reports and more similar features. If these are the features you're interested in, Asana would definitely be a better choice for you.

Motion is better suited for small teams (<50 employees). If you're reading this and thinking "I don't *really* care about reporting dashboards, I just want my team to better manage their projects, be more productive, organized and get work done on time" then Motion would definitely be a better pick for you.

To put things into perspective, after using Asana for 7 years, we switched off to Motion as we found a drastic time-saving component.

Winner: Motion

If you're looking for something new and innovative in the project management space that takes into account more than just the tasks you have in your project manager (e.g. team meetings, workload, team dependencies, time blocking, and more), Motion delivers.

We believe that Motion is actually redefining the entire project management space as a whole, which has otherwise been trending toward the all-in-one category (a vicious trap which brings with it a myriad of problems for which we are overall quite skeptical of).

This is made especially clear when comparing Motion vs Notion, Motion vs ClickUp, and Motion vs Monday. As instead of focusing purely on project management (similar to that of Asana), the rest are getting sidetracked in other categories like team knowledge base and even CRM. 🤦

The task/project management space is due for some innovation, especially with the rise of AI, and only thing we know for sure is that it's not going to happen from the software we just mentioned as they think they've already "won" project management...

That said, we can't help but think that Motion has a genuine shot at actually evolving the space as a whole, as they've been quietly building in the background and have the right team and foundation to completely disrupt what we currently think of when we hear the term "Project Management". If you use Motion, I think you might see the glimpse into the future like we have.

Start your 7 day free trial of Motion here.

Winner: Asana

If you don't care as much about AI or being on the cutting edge, and you value core traditional project management system functionality (e.g. reporting, gantt charts, and a well-refined and stable API), Asana is likely the option as this is where they excel.

It ultimately comes down to what key features you're looking for though. If time blocking or deep calendar integration is important, you're not going to get that with Asana. What you will get though is a deeply integrated and scaleable project management system.

Motion still has a bit more to go on some core functionality (e.g. super robust searching functionality), but that's definitely coming in time but these types of features are readily available in Asana.

In-fact, we personally used Asana in our business for 7 years. It was stable, had a great API (you can even see some of the cool things we've built by integrating Asana using their API), and worked well. While we ultimately made the move to Motion in recent years, that doesn't mean we no longer recommend Asana.

If you're a larger team that highly value stability and traditional project management core features over all else, the truth of the matter is that you can't wrong with Asana. Even when comparing Asana vs ClickUp, Asana vs Monday, Asana vs Notion, you're definitely getting the best project management software when going with Asana as compared to those all-in-one tools.

Start your free trial of Asana here.

Winner: Motion

Your team will enjoy using Motion over Asana as it's so deeply tied to their calendar, and is essentially a daily planner in addition to a project manager, right there within their calendar.

If you're truly looking at it from the individual's standpoint, they can simply live in Motion for their calendar + event scheduling, which in turn will help with adoption, versus jumping between multiple tools (Calendar/Calendly/Asana).

If you're a small to medium team and you can use Motion, we'd recommend it over Asana purely from the enjoyment and ease of use side of things.

Start your 7 day free trial of Motion here.

Winner: Asana‍

Motion is nowhere near that of Asana when you get outside of the "getting work done" category and move into "upper-management reporting".

If you're a team that's big into goal setting and milestones, while there's ways to visualize it by filtering down views in Motion and leveraging projects for different chunks of a project, you're not going to get the more traditional reporting functionality that upper-management might want.

We'll note that this is nearly irrelevant for the actual teams that are living in the tool on the day-to-day to get work done, so you'd have to ask yourself, is reporting more important for your team than your team actually using a tool and benefiting from it, to help them get work done?

That said if features like project management reporting, dashboards, timeline views, progress reports are core for upper management to see, then Asana would be better suited.

Start your free trial of Asana here.

Winner: Both

While there is a "free" tier to Asana, it's incredibly restrictive feature-wise (just about everything you try to do prompts you to upgrade), and if you have 15+ seats, you'll be forced to the Premium or Business tier. Since this site is more focused on B2B, we're going to discuss the pricing options in a realistic scenario where your company is looking to evaluate Motion vs Asana.

For small teams, Motion and Asana are nearly identical per seat as compared to Asana (paid annual). Not to mention, if you're using Asana long enough, you're going to be pulled to the Business tier—as they gate-keep many features behind it.

You also need to keep in mind the fact that Motion also includes a full calendar (a more powerful layer atop your Google Calendar/Outlook Calendar to view tasks and manage your schedule, and meeting scheduler. So this also replaces the need for most people using Calendly as well, which should free up some budget.

With either tool we recommend starting on the monthly tier for evaluation and within 2–3 months of implementing the tool (whether Motion OR Asana), you should switch to annual for the discount.

It's also a statement you're making to your team that you're committed to making the tool work. We find that larger teams that use a tool month-to-month have a difficult mindset around embracing the tool, as they feel like the company might just switch tools again the following month. Commit to the software and your team will also do the same (plus you get to save nearly 40% on the price by doing so).

Innovation
Innovation
Draw

Motion completely changes the game by making the calendar the heart of project management, not just an add-on. You get automatic task scheduling, AI-powered rescheduling, and the entire team's priorities shift in real time, no one else does this, and it's not even close. If you want something that genuinely redefines what project management can be, Motion is the clear standout.

Asana was a trendsetter in making project management simple and user-friendly and still leads with thoughtful, purposeful features, especially around AI. But its innovation is about refining what already exists, not inventing a totally new approach.

If you care most about true innovation and want something that feels fundamentally different, pick Motion. Asana is great for polished, user-friendly workflows, but Motion is the one actually pushing the boundaries.

AI Assistance
AI Assistance
Draw

Motion stands out for how deeply AI is woven into every part of the experience. Its assistant manages your schedule, auto-schedules tasks, and can answer questions about anything across your projects, meeting notes, or docs in a chat format. Everything feels like it's handled by an actual assistant, not just an add-on.

Asana's AI is well integrated and its chat lets you move projects and deadlines conversationally, which is genuinely helpful. The AI teammates feature is a unique touch since you can assign them tasks and they'll actually get real work done, but usage is capped unless you pay for more credits.

If you want the most time saved and the most built-in, always-on AI help across your project management workflow, Motion is the clear pick. Asana's AI is smooth and useful, especially for teams that want AI teammates, but Motion just does more out of the box and feels more like having a real assistant on your team.

Daily Focus
Daily Focus
Draw

If you want to know exactly what to work on next without any hassle, Motion makes it dead simple. You just check your calendar and get moving, no training or fiddling required. This is perfect if you're just trying to be productive and don't want to waste time managing your project management tool.

Asana has improved with its AI Chat and AI teammates, which help you stay on top of your work and make it easier to update things. But you still have to keep a close watch and manually babysit details, so it doesn't fully get out of your way.

If your main priority is actually managing your own daily work with zero friction, Motion is the clear pick here. Asana is getting better, but Motion just nails daily focus for individuals.

Ease of Learning
Ease of Learning
Best
Best

Motion is basically ready to use right away, and most teams can figure out everything they need in a couple of hours without outside help. You don't need to bring in an expert or spend days sorting out the setup. That makes it a great fit if you just want to get going fast without extra hassle.

Asana, even though it has a good user experience, is so flexible that you'll probably need someone dedicated to set it up properly. If you want to use its automation or advanced workflows, you're looking at extra training and possibly hiring an expert. Teams won't be up and running in less than a day unless they already have that help lined up.

If you want your team moving today, Motion is the clear pick. Asana only makes sense if you're willing to invest serious time or hire someone to manage the setup.

Team Adoption
Team Adoption
Best
Best

Motion is significantly easier for teams to adopt because everything is scheduled right onto each person's calendar, so people just open their calendar and know what to do next. That simplicity means teams are way more likely to actually use it and keep using it without extra steps.

Asana, on the other hand, needs a lot of setup before your team can even start using it. You'll have to create documentation, run training sessions, and keep enforcing the system from management just to get people onboarded. Teams often drop it unless someone's actively making sure everyone sticks with it, and it's easy for things to get confusing if the setup isn't perfect.

If you want your team to actually use the tool without a ton of friction or extra work, Motion is hands-down the better option.

### Motion vs Calendly
**Live Link**: [https://efficient.app/compare/motion-vs-calendly](https://efficient.app/compare/motion-vs-calendly)

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
Motion and Calendly are for entirely different scheduling needs. Motion is better if you have less than 10 meetings a week, and you also want a tool to help you manage projects, while Calendly is a better if scheduling is a part of your business processes (e.g. you need to accept payments, route leads to sales reps, etc).

That said, there is another tool that we recommend over Calendly if you fit into the latter category, and that tool is Cal.

Best Scheduling Software? (Calendly vs Cal.com vs SavvyCal vs Chili Piper vs TidyCal)

Best Scheduling Software? (Calendly vs Cal.com vs SavvyCal vs Chili Piper vs TidyCal)
Watch
24.8K views
Editor's Verdict
Editor's Verdict
Copy Link
Simplicity

Winner: Motion

I've tried a lot of calendar scheduling tools, have even changed our Efficient App software stack scheduler some 8 times over the past decade? And I must say that in terms of setting up Motion as a scheduler, having it check for team member (and personal calendar) conflicts, and just easily creating and sending over custom booking links to people, Motion has been the easiest to setup and maintain.

Even embedding booking pages onto your website is super seamless, and it just looks more modern and clean than that of even Calendly.

Start your 7 day free trial of Motion here.

Team Scheduling

Winner: Calendly

While Motion has great individual event scheduling functionality (with the ability to check against other team member availability), they aren't currently focused on team scheduling functionality.

So if you're looking to have shared team booking links, and other team-focused scheduling functionality, you're probably better off using a tool that is focusing on that right now, which is Calendly.

That said, if you're looking for more of an internal team AI scheduling tool (where your internal team events optimize themselves to find the most focus time for you and your team), that's where you'll want to check out either Clockwise or Reclaim, which is in a slightly different category of Time Management.

Try Calendly for free here.

Native Integrations

Winner: Calendly

Calendly takes the cake when it comes to actual scheduling-related integrations, allowing you to do things like requiring payment via Stripe before an appointment can be booked, and native integrations with other tools on the market.

Even deep integrations with your CRM by using a tool like Outfunnel which works out-of-the-box to add leads to your CRM upon appointment booking, along with logging appointment bookings and changes as activities, and even triggering updated fields upon booking being scheduled.

That said, there are workarounds available for some of these things with Motion, but key word "workarounds". So take for example the requiring payment before booking example. With Motion, you can book in a paid consultation with us, which we simply take payment via Stripe Checkout, and then redirect you to our booking page. Worst case scenario, someone books in without paying, and we'd know and just cancel the appointment. So all-in-all, at our size, these things are not a huge deal.

But if you just want more things to work out of the box, no workarounds required, then you're probably better off using a tool like Calendly if you want to have super fine-grained control over your booking tool.

I will also note that currently, Motion does not have a scheduler API currently, so if you want to integrate the scheduler, you need to be clever using a tool like Mailparser and Zapier to accomplish some of this functionality (which we have done for some of our customers).

Try Calendly for free here.

Motion vs Calendly Overall Value

Winner: Motion

If you're on the free tier of Calendly, then they win on the value front, although you're getting quite limited functionality. So for this category, let's just assume you're actually on a paid tier of Calendly to keep things fair.

Motion for us replaced the need for Chili Piper, Asana, and Vimcal, so in terms of overall value, we're actually paying less overall for Motion, and getting 95% of what we needed from these other tools, all in a cohesive (yet purposeful) solution.

So if you're in a similar boat, and the Scheduler, Calendar, and Project Management solution that Motion offers can replace the other tools you use in the category, it's a no-brainer on overall value. Especially if you're looking for more than just a meeting scheduler, and you're looking to block time from things like important tasks you need to get done, and you'd like how the time blocks actually affect when people should be able to schedule in with you. Allowing you to actually get more focus work done in your day.

Start your 7 day free trial of Motion here.

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
Motion and Calendly are for entirely different scheduling needs. Motion is better if you have less than 10 meetings a week, and you also want a tool to help you manage projects, while Calendly is a better if scheduling is a part of your business processes (e.g. you need to accept payments, route leads to sales reps, etc).

If you need advanced scheduling workflows like round robin lead routing or deep Salesforce integration, Chili Piper is built for that and handles those enterprise sales team needs way better than Motion. Chili Piper is focused on robust lead routing and enterprise workflows, so if your team is sales-heavy and lives in Salesforce, pick Chili Piper.

But if you want a scheduler that's flexible, easy to use, and integrated with your project management and task workflows, Motion is the clear choice. Motion is way better for individuals or smaller teams who care more about spinning up links fast, blocking out focus time, or organizing tasks alongside meetings. It's not built for complex routing or payment collection, but it nails the basics and is much more adaptable if you don't need enterprise-level features.

So if you're at a big sales org using Salesforce and need heavy-duty lead routing, go Chili Piper. For everyone else, especially if you want scheduling that fits right into your daily workflow, Motion is the standout.

### Motion vs ClickUp
**Live Link**: [https://efficient.app/compare/motion-vs-clickup](https://efficient.app/compare/motion-vs-clickup)

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
Motion and ClickUp have two entirely different project management philosophies. ClickUp is an all-in-one tool, trying to be everything from task management to docs to whiteboards. While they've had marketing, many teams have complained that their tool falls short when it comes to performance and UX.

Motion is a project management tool with a calendar at the core. It helps teams get more work done by using AI to auto-schedule tasks on your calendar (like a built in assistant/project manager).

Innovation
Innovation

Motion is in a different league when it comes to innovation. It's the only project management tool here that actually rethinks how things should work, building everything around a calendar so tasks are scheduled and rescheduled for you automatically. That core design is unique and goes way beyond just adding features for show.

ClickUp tries to stand out by being an all-in-one platform, but most of its features feel stitched together instead of truly reimagining project management. The ambition is there, but the follow-through isn't.

If you care about using something that genuinely changes how project management works, Motion is the clear pick. ClickUp just doesn't push the boundaries in the same way.

AI Assistance
AI Assistance
Best
Best

Motion is on a different level for AI assistance. Its AI isn't just there for show, it actually runs your schedule, auto-schedules tasks, and acts like a real assistant you can ask about anything in your projects, team, or docs. The Motion assistant pulls info from everywhere and gives you direct answers, plus it has AI note taking and docs built right in.

ClickUp's AI, on the other hand, just doesn't get much use. People like that it exists, but most don't actually rely on it to get work done. It's more of an extra than something that saves real time.

If you actually want AI that helps you move faster and takes work off your plate, Motion is the clear pick here. ClickUp's AI won't change your workflow much, but Motion's will.

Daily Focus
Daily Focus
Best
Best

If you want to know exactly what to do next without any hassle, Motion makes it dead simple. You just check your calendar and get to work, with no real learning curve or setup headaches. It's built for people who want to dive in and focus, not fiddle around.

ClickUp, on the other hand, might have a lot of features, but it's easy to get lost and waste time just trying to find the right screen. The extra steps and clutter cause frustration and slow you down.

If daily focus matters to you, Motion is the obvious pick. It keeps things clear and actionable, while ClickUp just adds noise and friction.

Ease of Learning
Ease of Learning
Best
Best

Motion takes the hassle out of getting started. You can go from zero to fully set up in a couple of hours, no agency or specialist needed. It's made for small to medium-sized teams who just want to get moving, not spend weeks figuring things out.

ClickUp is on the opposite end. It's so customizable that setup becomes a project in itself. Unless you're ready to hire an agency or assign a dedicated person just to wrangle the tool, you'll hit a steep learning curve right away.

If you want your team up and running today without extra help, Motion is the clear pick. ClickUp only makes sense if you have the time and resources to invest in heavy configuration.

Team Adoption
Team Adoption
Best
Best

Motion stands out for team adoption because it's dead simple to pick up, everyone just checks their calendar to see what's next, so people actually use it without hassle or extra steps.

ClickUp, on the other hand, is much more work to get going. Teams need real training and setup before anyone gets comfortable, and if you skip that, most people won't bother using it.

If you want your whole team to actually use a project management tool without fighting through onboarding or setup, Motion is the clear choice here.

If you want true time management that actually plans your work and calendar together, Motion is the only real choice. Clockwise only makes sense if all you need is to shuffle internal meetings for a big team, and even then, you'll be frustrated if you want anything beyond that.

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
Motion actually feels like having a personal assistant that builds your day using your team's projects, tasks, and deadlines, while Cluely is mostly known for its marketing and feels less finished.

Only use Cluely if you want something unique and don't mind rough edges, but Motion gets you working faster and just works better day to day.

Best AI meeting bot for task creation

Editor's Verdict

Motion actually handles meeting notes and documentation in a way that feels built-in and useful for project management. Its AI Docs and Meeting Recorder don't just capture what was said, they instantly tie notes and tasks back to your projects, so action items never get lost and you can see everything in one place. Tasks are created automatically from meetings, and you can approve or assign them without extra steps.

Cluely, in contrast, is mostly an AI chat overlay that suggests questions and context during calls. It's active and persistent, but it doesn't integrate meeting notes or documentation back into a broader project workflow. Its real advantage is surfacing info in the moment, but once the call's over, you're left with a transcript and some notes, not a living record that drives your next steps.

If you care about meeting notes and docs that actually move your projects forward, Motion is the clear pick. Cluely's approach is more about real-time suggestions, but it doesn't match Motion's depth or integration for capturing, connecting, and acting on meeting outputs.

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
Motion acts like a personal assistant by using AI to organize your tasks, projects, and schedule, while Fireflies feels cluttered and buggy unless you really need advanced meeting audio analysis and love sorting soundbites.

Only use Fireflies if you specifically want deep meeting audio tools and don't mind complexity; stick with Motion for a more streamlined, AI-driven workday.

Motion nails meeting recording for teams who want a recorder that just works in the background and automatically ties meeting notes, tasks, and project details together. Its meeting recorder joins your calls, records everything, then uses AI to organize notes and even creates actionable tasks you can assign right away. You barely have to touch it, and it connects directly to your ongoing projects so all your meeting content is immediately useful and in context.

Fireflies, on the other hand, piles on a ton of meeting recording features and deep integrations, but the experience is overwhelming unless you're the kind of person or team who loves manually organizing call snippets and playlists. Even basic video recording is locked behind the top pricing tier, and you constantly run into upsells and storage limits.

If you want a meeting recorder that disappears into your workflow and helps your team get actual work done with minimal effort, Motion is the clear pick. Only go with Fireflies if you specifically need those unique audio snippet tools and are okay managing the added complexity. For most teams, Motion is simply easier, more integrated, and far less hassle.

Motion builds your day with AI by organizing tasks, deadlines, and meetings into your calendar, while Gong analyzes sales calls and emails to show what's working in enterprise sales.

Motion blows Gong out of the water when it comes to actually managing projects and automating your day-to-day work. Gong, on the other hand, is all about analyzing sales activity for big sales teams. It records calls, emails, and meetings, then runs AI to flag risks and suggest next steps in deals. **Live Link**: [https://efficient.app/compare/motion-vs-google-calendar](https://efficient.app/compare/motion-vs-google-calendar)

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
Google Calendar is a great free calendar...but it's just that, a calendar. Motion is a layer on-top of Google Calendar and helps you manage your tasks and work using AI to plan your day (like an assistant would).

Best Scheduling Software? (Calendly vs Cal.com vs SavvyCal vs Chili Piper vs TidyCal)

Editor's Verdict
Editor's Verdict
Main Category
Main Category
Copy Link

Comparing Motion to Google Calendar isn't a straightforward apples-to-apples scenario.

Calendar Functionality

Check out the app screenshots above to better understand what you're going to get on the calendar side of things, because they are nearly identical, it's just more about how it looks.

Motion does add in some nice quality of life features though like Travel Time (for before/after meetings):

Easily add Travel Time before/after the event in 15 minute intervals

Scheduler

This is where Motion stands out over Google Calendar, as a lot of thought went into their meeting scheduler. You can set it to check against team calendar availability and even generate the meeting request in plain text, making it feel less like a meeting URL:

Create plain-text meeting scheduler requests that check against team calendar availability

Google Calendar vs Motion

Many of us are already familiar with Google Calendar – it's a free tool that is used as the foundation and back-end for not just Motion but for numerous other daily planner apps. Google Calendar has all the essential features you'd expect from a reliable calendar, fulfilling its primary function.

The cracks in Google Calendar's facade begin to show when delving into its additional features, such as Google Tasks or Appointment Scheduling.

While these functionalities exist, they are more so afterthoughts, lacking in a great (or even good) user experience. Users tend to find these features clunky and inefficient, requiring excessive clicks and navigation, which can lead to frustration and a sense of limitation within the Google Calendar ecosystem. And that's when you might start looking elsewhere... 👀

Motion vs Google Calendar

Enter Motion: a tool that is more than just a calendar.

Motion's strength lies in its sophisticated meeting scheduler and task manager, both of which are powered by AI. This advanced technology is adept at organizing tasks based on their priority, offering a level of personalized efficiency that Google Calendar cannot match.

Users of Motion report feeling like they have an AI assistant at their disposal when using Motion. This sets Motion apart and is a reason why many are willing to pay for a better calendar + task tool.

Which Should I Use? Motion vs Google Calendar

The silly answer here is, well... Both—you need a proper calendar like Google Calendar if you're to use a tool like Motion to its fullest potential. There's also a few instances where the Google Calendar API is actually limited (for every calendar in the market) for things like updating team calendar events (that you aren't the owner of), so you'll be occasionally jumping into Google Calendar in some edge-cases like that. Other than that, Motion would be your full-time calendar with Google Calendar just being the infrastructure on the background for events and calendar functionality.

**Live Link**: [https://efficient.app/compare/motion-vs-granola](https://efficient.app/compare/motion-vs-granola)

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
Motion actually feels like having a personal AI assistant that manages your team's tasks, schedules, and priorities for you, while Granola just helps you remember meetings and offers call context without needing a meeting bot.

Only use Granola if you need an always-on meeting memory and context tool, but pick Motion if you want AI to actively organize and drive your daily work.

Motion completely takes over your daily planning by auto-scheduling tasks onto your calendar, so you start every day with a clear, actionable plan. If you want to stop thinking about what to do next and just get to work, Motion actually eliminates manual planning and the constant shuffling of tasks. It's built for people who want to finish meaningful work, not just tinker with productivity setups or endlessly reorganize.

Granola, on the other hand, is all about meetings. It acts like an always-on, invisible note-taking assistant, giving you perfect recall of what happened in every meeting and letting you ask questions about the meeting context, even mid-call. It's lightweight, doesn't interrupt calls, and is basically your go-to for making meetings more useful and the details retrievable.

If your main pain point is getting your day mapped and tasks scheduled so you actually do the work, Motion is the obvious pick. But if you live in meetings and want a way to capture, recall, and interact with meeting content without bots or interruptions, Granola is the better fit. Each one crushes its own lane, but they solve totally different efficiency problems. Pick Motion if you want your whole workday planned with zero effort. Choose Granola if you need a flawless meeting memory and real-time meeting help.

Innovation
Innovation
Best
Best

Motion completely outpaces Jira on innovation. Motion isn't just another project manager with a calendar bolted on - it's built around a calendar-first approach, letting you schedule tasks directly and automatically reshuffling your team's workload with AI when things shift. That kind of deep integration and automatic rescheduling just doesn't exist anywhere else.

Jira, on the other hand, is described as stagnant and stuck on old tech. There's no mention of anything new or redefining about how it handles projects, and it's called out as a poor fit for modern teams.

If you care about using something that actually rethinks project management and isn't just more of the same, Motion is the only real choice here. Jira doesn't even come close.

AI Assistance
AI Assistance
Best
Best

Motion absolutely runs circles around Jira when it comes to genuinely useful AI features. Motion's AI is baked into everything: it actually auto-schedules your tasks like a real assistant, answers project and company questions by pulling info from your docs and meeting notes, and even offers AI note taking and document creation. This stuff saves real time, not just clicks.

Jira's AI, on the other hand, is pretty bare bones. It mostly covers the basics like summarizing, search, and writing, and the way it's set up feels clunky. There's nothing in the review saying it actually changes your workflow or acts like an assistant.

If you want AI that actually helps you get work done and feels like part of your team, go with Motion. Jira's AI is just there so they can say they have it, but it won't move the needle.

Daily Focus
Daily Focus
Best
Best

If you care about actually staying focused on your own work, Motion is in a completely different league from Jira. With Motion, you just open it and instantly know what to tackle next, since everything is built around your calendar. There's barely any setup or learning curve, so you can be productive right away without getting lost in menus or process.

Jira, on the other hand, is mostly built for managers tracking progress and generating reports. If you're an individual contributor, it actually slows you down, people complain about losing time just trying to keep it updated for someone else. It's clunky and feels like extra overhead instead of helping you focus.

So if you want a tool that genuinely helps you manage your own day-to-day work, go with Motion. Jira just isn't made for that.

Ease of Learning
Ease of Learning
Best
Best

Motion is basically ready to use right away, with only light setup and a learning curve that takes maybe a couple of hours. Teams can get started the same day without needing outside help or dedicated setup people.

Jira is the opposite. The learning curve is steep enough that there's a whole certification just to figure it out, and it's really meant for enterprise teams that have someone focused full-time on managing the tool. If you want to get started quickly, Jira will slow you down.

If your team needs something they can pick up and run with immediately, Motion is the clear choice. Only go with Jira if you already have someone dedicated to managing a complex system and are ready for a heavy onboarding process.

Team Adoption
Team Adoption
Best
Best

Motion is dramatically easier for teams to adopt because it puts tasks right on each person's calendar, so everyone instantly knows what to do next without extra effort. That directness means teams actually use it and stick with it.

Jira, on the other hand, is almost always forced on teams by management, and most people actively dislike using it. The frustration is so bad that people even think about quitting over it.

If you care about your team actually using the tool and not resenting it, Motion is the clear choice.

Innovation
Innovation
Draw

Motion completely changes the game on innovation by making the calendar the heart of project management, not just an add-on. The way it schedules and reschedules tasks for the whole team using AI, and the fact that no one else comes close to this level of integration, makes it stand out. If you want something that genuinely redefines how project management works, especially around time and scheduling, Motion is the clear choice.

Linear is impressive for engineers, modernizing old workflows and integrating with coding tools, but it's more about upgrading the experience for its audience than truly reinventing the category. Motion is the one actually pushing boundaries and doing things you won't find anywhere else. If innovation is what you care about most, go with Motion.

AI Assistance
AI Assistance
Draw

Motion takes the lead for AI assistance in project management. Its AI isn't just a bolt-on, it's at the heart of how you use the app, automatically building your schedule and slotting in tasks like a real assistant. You can ask the built-in Motion assistant pretty much anything about your projects, docs, or team, and get answers instantly. The AI note taker and AI docs go further, pulling info together so you don't have to dig.

Linear's AI features are genuinely useful too, with smart issue summaries, backlog prioritization, and even automatic sprint reports. It's especially strong at handling ticket triage and integrates tightly with engineering tools, plus it can create issues from Slack chats. But the catch is, all this power is designed specifically for engineering teams, if you're not in that world, it's just not for you.

So if you're looking for AI to help manage all kinds of projects and want a true assistant experience, Motion is the clear pick. If you're running an engineering team and need AI that speaks your language, Linear's worth a look, but for most people, Motion's AI will save you more time and fit your workflow better.

Daily Focus
Daily Focus
Draw

Motion makes daily focus effortless. Its calendar-centered approach means you always know what to tackle next, and there's basically zero learning curve. You can dive in and immediately see your priorities, which is perfect if you want to get stuff done without any setup or confusion.

Linear is also strong for daily focus, especially for engineering teams. Its clean design, speed, and keyboard shortcuts keep things moving smoothly day to day. But it's more tailored for developers and people who appreciate a streamlined, technical workflow.

If you want the simplest, most direct way to know what to work on each day, Motion is the clear pick. It's built around daily clarity for anyone, not just engineers, and you don't have to think twice about what's next. Linear is great if you're a developer and love minimal, fast tools, but Motion is the one to grab for instant daily focus.

Ease of Learning
Ease of Learning
Draw

If your team is managing engineering tasks, Linear is super easy to pick up because the interface is minimal, and the workflows use engineering best practices right out of the box. You'll get started fast, but only if you're actually doing engineering work. If you aren't, Linear's terminology and setup feel clunky, and you'll probably end up fighting the tool or working around its defaults.

Motion, on the other hand, is designed for small to medium-sized teams who just want to get going without any special setup or hiring outside help. The learning curve is basically flat, everything you need is clear within a couple hours, and you're not forced to adapt to any niche workflow or jargon.

So, if you're an engineering team, Linear feels just as quick to get started. For everyone else, Motion is the clear pick because you won't hit any weird roadblocks or have to translate the tool to fit your needs. Both are fast to learn, but Motion is a safer bet unless you're squarely in the engineering camp.

Team Adoption
Team Adoption
Draw

Linear makes onboarding for engineering teams almost instant, with zero training needed and deep integration with coding tools, Slack, and even the terminal. Engineers pick it up right away and actually enjoy using it, which never happens with new software. But it's only a smooth ride if your team is engineering, product, or design, marketing and sales will hit a wall and probably bounce to something else, so adoption drops hard if you need everyone on board.

Motion, on the other hand, is super easy for any team to start using since it just puts tasks straight onto everyone's calendar. That calendar-first setup means people always know what's next, so there's way less friction and much higher odds the whole team sticks with it, regardless of their role.

If your team is mostly engineers or product folks, Linear is almost impossible to beat for adoption. But if you need everyone from marketing to sales on the same tool, Motion is a much safer bet for getting the whole team actually using it, and staying on it.

Motion is in a different league when it comes to innovation. It's the only project management tool here that actually rethinks the basics, building everything around a calendar-first system and using AI to automatically schedule and reschedule tasks for the whole team. That core integration isn't just a feature bolted on later, it's the foundation, and nobody else offers anything close.

If you care about using something genuinely new that changes how project management can work, Motion is the clear pick. Monday just isn't in the conversation on this one.

AI Assistance
AI Assistance

Motion blows Monday out of the water when it comes to genuinely useful AI assistance. Motion's AI isn't just bolted on - it's at the core of how the app works, handling scheduling, auto-prioritizing tasks, answering project questions, and even pulling info from meeting notes and docs in a chat. The assistant actually feels like a timesaving partner, not a gimmick.

Monday's AI, on the other hand, feels like a half-finished layer that doesn't make a real dent in your workload. Daily Focus
Daily Focus

Best

Monday makes you jump through more hoops. Even after setup and training, adoption is a pain, and team members often end up relying on a project manager to keep everything updated. That slows everyone down and works against individual focus.

Monday is way tougher to learn. If your goal is to get your team up and running in less than a day, Motion is the clear pick. Monday just takes too much time and effort for most teams.

Team Adoption
Team Adoption

Motion makes team adoption almost effortless. Since it puts tasks straight onto each person's calendar, everyone knows exactly what to do next without extra steps or training. That built-in simplicity means teams are way more likely to actually start using it and stick with it over time.

Monday, on the other hand, really depends on someone setting it up right and making sure everyone gets trained. If you skip onboarding or the initial setup is off, your team probably won't use it at all.

AI Auto-Scheduling
AI Auto-Scheduling
Best
Best

Motion actually does AI auto-scheduling the way you want: your tasks get dropped onto your calendar automatically, priorities and deadlines are handled for you, and if anything shifts, Motion just updates your entire day without you lifting a finger. You never have to worry about keeping your schedule accurate or reorganizing after a missed task. It's set-and-forget, and you always know what to do next.

Morgen Calendar doesn't do any of that. You still have to manually drag and drop every task, and if your plans change, you're stuck reshuffling everything by hand. There's talk of a future Dynamic Time Blocking feature, but right now, there's zero AI-driven scheduling. This might be tolerable if you only have a few tasks, but as soon as things get busy, the lack of automation becomes a real headache.

If you actually want your planner to build and rebuild your day for you, Motion is the only real choice here. Morgen Calendar just can't keep up when it comes to AI auto-scheduling.

Adaptive Rescheduling
Adaptive Rescheduling
Best
Best

Motion is in a completely different league for adaptive rescheduling. The moment anything changes, missed tasks, new meetings, shifting deadlines, Motion instantly reorganizes your entire schedule without you lifting a finger. You never have to drag things around or re-prioritize when life gets chaotic. It just keeps you on track, flags problems, and makes sure nothing slips, no matter how messy things get.

Morgen Calendar, on the other hand, leaves all the heavy lifting to you. If your plans shift, you have to manually move every task yourself. Nothing updates automatically, so your schedule gets stale unless you constantly babysit it. This might be fine if you only have a handful of tasks, but as soon as things get complicated, the workflow falls apart.

If you want a planner that actually adapts and saves you from constant manual updates, Motion is the only real choice here. Morgen just can't keep up.

Cognitive Clarity
Cognitive Clarity
Best
Best

Motion completely takes the mental load off by automatically handling your priorities, scheduling, and constant reshuffling as things change, so you never have to worry about what's next or whether you're overbooked. It flags risks, updates timelines, and always surfaces what matters most, giving you one clear view of your day, week, or projects without you having to think about it.

Morgen Calendar looks cleaner at first glance, but it leaves you to do all the heavy lifting. You have to manually sort, prioritize, and move tasks around. If your day shifts or things pile up, you're stuck reorganizing everything yourself, and clutter creeps in fast as your task load grows.

If you want real cognitive clarity and less mental clutter, Motion is the obvious pick. It actually simplifies your life instead of just looking simple. Morgen Calendar is only workable if you have very little to track and don't mind doing all the thinking yourself.

Planning Methodology
Planning Methodology
Best
Best

Motion makes planning almost automatic. Everything centers on the calendar, and tasks, meetings, and priorities all flow from there. The AI handles scheduling and updates in the background, so you don't waste time moving things around or worrying about missed tasks. You can jump in fast, especially if you're a busy professional or a small team, and keyboard shortcuts plus instant project templates keep things moving. The only real downside is occasional interface clutter, but you always know what to do next and never have to fight with the app.

Morgen Calendar is easy to start with and feels smooth if you just want basic, personal planning. You can drag tasks around and won't get lost looking for features, but it's limited. There's no automation, deeper organization, or real structure for work needs. If you want to keep on top of a lot, you're stuck doing everything manually, and it gets messy fast as your needs grow.

If you want a planner that's quick to pick up but also handles complexity and keeps you organized without constant effort, Motion is the clear choice. Morgen Calendar is fine for simple, personal use, but Motion actually gets out of your way and keeps everything running smoothly, especially when plans change.

Integrations
Integrations
Draw

Morgen Calendar gives you direct, native integrations with the big calendar platforms and a few popular project management tools, so syncing your main tasks and events is straightforward if you use those supported apps. It's not a full plug-and-play setup and the integration list is pretty short, but at least you can centralize the basics without jumping through too many hoops.

Motion falls behind here. You only get two native options (email and Siri), and even the new API and Zapier integration are missing key pieces like custom fields and don't touch the appointment scheduling side at all. That means you're stuck manually moving info, or hacking together workarounds, especially if you want to keep your CRM or meeting scheduling in sync.

If you're looking for a daily planner that actually connects to your main tools without constant manual effort, Morgen is the safer pick. It covers more ground out of the box and is less likely to break your flow, even if it's not perfect for power users. Motion just isn't there yet unless you only need the most basic integrations.

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
Notion isn't a proper project management tool, so if you're looking for that, the clear winner is Motion.

The most common outreach we get is actually from teams who have tried using Notion as their all-in-one tool (covering project/task management), and in every single case, they are overwhelmed and struggling with team adoption.

Notion is a team knowledge base, not a project management tool.

Don't let Notion's marketing and template community convince you otherwise—believe us, we understand the temptation.

If you're wondering what is the difference between Motion and Notion then you're likely looking at it from the project management standpoint, since Motion is actually quite different from Notion (which was initially created as a team knowledge base).

That said, since Notion has recently started advertising itself as having full project management capabilities, people have started looking into if the claim holds water.

So while both Motion and Notion technically provide task management and project management capabilities, Notion is not proper project management software. After reading this article, you will know exactly which tool better suits your needs! ⤵

The main difference between Motion and Notion is that Motion is for individuals and teams who are looking for more of an "assistant" to help manage their time. It's a full blown project manager with AI capabilities, that automatically re-arranges your tasks on your calendar based on priority and schedule. Didn't complete a task? No worries! Motion will automatically reschedule missed tasks for you during your next available timeslot.

Notion on the other hand is becoming more and more an all-in-one tool which is first-and-foremost a note taking and team knowledge base tool, but since it has an unstructured database functionality baked in like that of Airtable vs Notion, it often gets used for more than what it was initially built for.

The truth of the matter is if you're deep into the world of Notion for internal documentation and note taking (like many are), and you're thinking about expanding it to managing your team's tasks as well, we'd recommend you instead use the Motion API (or Motion Zapier integration) and integrate Notion with Motion if absolutely critical. Or rather, ask yourself if your project management tool really needs to be that deeply integrated with Notion at all.

You're probably getting a sense of where these two tools stack up, so let's get into standalone categories to explore how they stack up with one another.

When looking at Motion vs Notion solely from the best project management software standpoint, they are night-and-day differences. Motion was built from the ground-up as task and project management tool that has deep integration with your calendar, time blocking capabilities, and AI scheduling functionality.

With Motion, you can manage full-fledged projects that are months in duration with a series of sub-tasks. You can assign tasks to different team members and indicate if there are blockers in the way of task completion and so forth. If you have large projects that you like to schedule way in advance, Motion would definitely be a better fit for you.

If it's a smaller task or project, simply set the priority, duration, and any dependencies, and watch it build the perfect schedule for you and your team to ensure all of your work is done at the most optimal time. It's the closest thing we've seen to an AI assistant. Miss your work for the day? It's all rescheduled to tomorrow, don't even need to lift a finger.

Notion on the other hand, just because it has an underlying database structure and templating functionality, does not give it any of the innovative functionality that Motion brings with it. When using Notion, it very much feels like you're using a note taking app to manage tasks and projects.

So while it may be nice to have one of the most powerful note taking tools on the market live alongside your projects, you're going to feel frustrated in the day-to-day scheduling your tasks in Notion, and lose out on the knowledge that is gained by having such deep calendar integration and time blocking capabilities, like that of a proper project management tool like Motion.

The truth of the matter is that we find ourselves constantly scratching our heads when we see people try to use Notion as a project manager. Maybe if you're the only one using it? But even then, just because you can, should you? We'd say no.

Motion is not trying to be your team knowledge management tool, they are clearly focused on the project management functionality more than anything. So while it has note taking and documentation functionality within projects and tasks (similar to that of Motion vs Asana), they aren't trying to be a team knowledge base, whereas Notion is (and we actually believe that this is where Notion specifically excels).

So if team notes is important to you, you're going to want to pick from the best knowledge base software. It's not uncommon to use that along with a proper project manager. Now if you're set on having your team knowledge base deeply intertwined with your project management tool, you're going to want to compare ClickUp vs Notion (although we don't recommend this path).

So point-blank, when comparing Motion and Notion for note taking functionality, you'd be better off using Notion.

If you're looking for something new and innovative in the project management space that takes into account more than just the tasks you have in your project manager (e.g. team meetings, workload, team dependencies, time blocking, and more), Motion delivers.

We believe that Motion is actually redefining the entire project management space as a whole, which has otherwise been trending toward the all-in-one category (a vicious trap which brings with it a myriad of problems for which we are overall quite skeptical of).

This is made especially clear when comparing Motion vs Notion, Motion vs ClickUp, and Motion vs Monday. As instead of focusing purely on project management (similar to that of Asana), the rest are getting sidetracked in other categories like team knowledge base and even CRM. 🤦

The task/project management space is due for some innovation, especially with the rise of AI, and only thing we know for sure is that it's not going to happen from the software we just mentioned as they think they've already "won" project management...

That said, we can't help but think that Motion has a genuine shot at actually evolving the space as a whole, as they've been quietly building in the background and have the right team and foundation to completely disrupt what we currently think of when we hear the term "Project Management". If you use Motion, I think you might see the glimpse into the future like we have.

Innovation
Innovation
Best
Best

Motion is the only tool here that rethinks the entire project mangement experience, making the calendar the core of how projects are managed, not just an afterthought. Its automatic scheduling and AI-driven rearrangement of tasks set a new standard, no other app does this, and it makes team coordination radically smoother.

Notion, while once innovative for letting users build their own systems, doesn't push project management forward in a way that helps most people. It's flexible, but that flexibility turns into a distraction for anyone who isn't deep into custom setups. Unless you're part of the small group that loves building everything from scratch, Notion just feels like extra work.

If you want something that actually changes how project management works, Motion is the clear pick. Notion's innovation is impressive for customization, but it doesn't move the needle for project management itself.

AI Assistance
AI Assistance
Best
Best

With Motion, AI is built into everything, from auto-scheduling your tasks like a real assistant to answering your project and company questions by pulling from all your notes, docs, and meetings. You actually save time using it, not just see AI features sitting there.

Notion has AI Agents that look fun but just don't deliver. People are mostly let down by how limited and underwhelming the features feel, so you're not getting much actual help.

If you care about genuinely useful AI that saves you time in project management, Motion is the only real choice here.

Daily Focus
Daily Focus
Best
Best

Motion makes it dead simple to know what to work on next, with almost no learning curve. You just check your calendar and get moving, so it's perfect if you want to stay productive without fussing over setup.

Notion, on the other hand, slows you down because you have to build your own system and learn database concepts just to track daily work. For most people managing their own tasks, it ends up being a distraction instead of helping you focus.

If you want an app that actually helps you get your own work done every day, Motion is the clear pick. Notion just isn't built for daily focus unless you're ready to do a lot of extra work yourself.

Ease of Learning
Ease of Learning
Best
Best

Motion is basically ready to go right away, with barely any setup or learning needed. Most teams can get the hang of it in an hour or two, no outside help required.

Notion, on the other hand, is only easy for people who already enjoy diving into databases and building things from scratch. For everyone else, the learning curve is much steeper and can be pretty frustrating, especially if you just want to manage projects without becoming an expert.

If your team wants to get started fast without extra hassle, Motion is the clear pick. Notion might work if you love tinkering, but for most people, it's just way harder to learn.

Team Adoption
Team Adoption
Best
Best

Motion makes team adoption almost effortless because tasks show up right on each person's calendar, so everyone knows what to do next without needing extra explanation. That built-in clarity means teams actually use it and stick with it.

Notion, on the other hand, puts all the pressure on whoever sets it up. You have to invent your own system, and it usually only makes sense to you, which leads to confusion and frustration when trying to get the rest of the team onboard. As the team grows, this only gets messier.

If you want your whole team to actually use the tool and keep using it, Motion is the clear choice. Notion just creates too much friction for most teams to ever get full adoption.

If you want a calendar that actively manages your time and tasks for you, Motion is in a league of its own. Its AI auto-scheduling doesn't just block out your day, it constantly rearranges tasks if you miss something, flags when you're at risk of missing deadlines, and adapts to team schedules. You show up every day knowing exactly what to work on next, and it keeps you honest about what's actually possible with your available time. This is for people who want their calendar to do more than just display events, it's a full assistant for busy professionals and teams who need everything in one place.

Notion Calendar (Cron) is all about the feel and flow of a classic calendar. The UI is gorgeous, it's fast, and the shortcuts make it a pleasure to use. If you just want a beautifully designed, free calendar that's better than Google Calendar for event scheduling and viewing, this is the clear pick. But it won't manage your workload, reschedule tasks, or help you keep up with project deadlines. It's not interested in being a task manager or productivity engine.

If your priority is a calendar that actually manages your time, keeps you on track with tasks, and helps you avoid falling behind, Motion is the obvious choice. If you only need a clean, fast, and thoughtfully designed calendar for events, Notion Calendar is the better fit.

Motion's meeting recorder is tightly integrated with its project management features, letting you automatically turn meeting notes into actionable tasks and link everything back to your projects. This means after a call, you get organized notes, instant task creation, and all your documentation stays connected without extra steps.

Otter is much simpler. It's great for just recording or transcribing a conversation, like at a doctor's appointment or for personal reference, but it stops there. If you want meeting notes that actually drive your work forward and cut out manual follow-up, Motion is the clear pick for business teams. Otter is only worth considering if all you need is a basic recording or transcription tool for personal use.

Editor's Verdict
Editor's Verdict
Main Category
Main Category
Copy Link
Which is Better? Motion vs Reclaim AI

If you're exploring Motion and Reclaim you are likely looking for an AI calendar that has deep calendar sync, smart scheduling, and focus time/time blocking capabilities as you want to better manage your time, plan your day, and get more focus work in.

While both Motion and Reclaim deliver on these capabilities, they are actually completely different tools with different focuses. After reading this article, you should know exactly which tool is best suited for your needs! ⤵

Motion vs Reclaim AI Differences

Motion was built to be your team's project management software and AI calendar. So with Motion, you shouldn't need to be opening up Google Calendar, or using any of the top calendar software on the market, because Motion is on the top of that list as well.

Reclaim on the other hand was built to be more of a time management assistant, meaning it will work in the background to help your team find the best time slots to work together for internal meetings (similar to that of Reclaim vs Clockwise). Reclaim though has incredibly basic task management functionality, and was never meant to be the project management tool for your team.

Task & Project Management

Winner: Motion

If you're looking for a proper task/project management tool, Reclaim is sure to let you down. Of the two tools, Motion actually has an impressive prioritization algorithm, that helps you prioritize the most important work to get done every day.

Motion has a myriad of prioritization tools, like priority, deadline, and a special AI algorithm that takes all of that into account when planning your day.

Instead, Reclaim integrates with your project management tools like Asana, ClickUp, Linear, and Jira. While you can now add prioritization on tasks, and Reclaim will automatically schedule the tasks on your calendar, the functionality isn’t anywhere near as robust as Motion.

And because we've gotten this question before—yes, Motion has habits (one of Reclaim's popular features) as well, because when you really think about it, habits are just recurring tasks with additional schedule restrictions (mornings, weekends, etc).

Start your 7 day free trial for Motion today.

Calendar

Motion allows you to integrate your personal and work calendar, so long as you're using Google Workspace/Gmail or Outlook for each. While Reclaim does the same, you aren't going to actually be using Reclaim as your calendar, you're still going to be using a separate calendar app in the day-to-day, just checking Reclaim when wanting to add in tasks or reschedule internal meetings with team members.

Motion and Reclaim both have a deep calendar sync, but Motion goes a layer deeper than Reclaim by syncing not only tasks from Motion onto your Google/Microsoft Calendar, but actually swapping the time blocking events (tasks) between free and busy depending on priority and deadline of the task at hand. Something we've found exceptionally useful to ensure we actually get work done on time.

We actually find it quite strange when comparing Motion to Reclaim and Clockwise, as neither of the competitors have invested into their tool actually replacing the calendar, which feels quite important to thrive in this category.

Calendar Meeting Scheduler

For individual and basic team usage, this is Motion's bread and butter. They were actually solely a calendar and appointment scheduler before they even moved into the time management and task management space. People report Motion as being a 50–100% better Calendly for individual usage in-fact. For a more in-depth breakdown, you can check out our Motion vs Calendly comparison if you'd like to see how they compare, as if you're looking for deeper team scheduling features or deep appointment booking integration, Calendly might be a better fit.

That said, Motion lets you go incredibly granular on all your scheduling preferences, in-fact, Motion has smart time blocking around tasks, putting them in your work calendar to ensure that you get them done at the best time to complete by the deadline. That's where their smart scheduling feature comes into play, preventing people using the meeting scheduler link from booking over the focus time scheduled for your most important work. They've truly thought about how to improve the scheduler category, and they've done an exceptional job on that front.

We found that Reclaim has a quite barebones appointment scheduler that has a lot to be desired. Want to have a scheduler link that prioritizes meetings over unimportant tasks, but not critical ones? Can't do that. Want to add more complex questions (e.g. dropdown select fields), when someone is booking? Can't do that either.

Team Meeting Optimization

If you're instead someone that has tons of internal meetings on your calendar every day, and you need a tool that can help you find more focused time blocks, that's where Reclaim stands out.

This also gets into a slightly different category though, which introduces other competitors, so if team meeting optimization is something that you're looking for, you might want to refer to our Clockwise vs Reclaim comparison instead.‍

Mobile App

Reclaim doesn’t have a mobile app. With that, you can probably better understand why we say that Reclaim is limited in task management as adding tasks on the fly isn’t a super seamless experience (you can log-in through your phone browser, but it’s a pretty cumbersome experience).

With Motion, you have a full-fledged mobile app. If you’re an iOS user, you can even say to Siri “Add a task with medium priority by next Tuesday” and it will create the task in Motion for you.

Motion vs Reclaim AI Final Thoughts

In summary, when comparing Motion vs Reclaim, Motion is a full-fledged project management tool, a replacement for your calendar, helpful for team collaboration, and it auto-schedules tasks on your teams calendar. It works great for individuals, and you'll only get more out of it as your team grows.

Reclaim on the other hand is just a tool that can be added to your productivity suite, specifically if you're an employee at a larger company looking to defend more of your time.

So while on the surface Motion and Reclaim seem similar, they are targeting completely different people and needs.

### Motion vs Routine
**Live Link**: [https://efficient.app/compare/motion-vs-routine](https://efficient.app/compare/motion-vs-routine)

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
While Motion and Routine both fall into the daily planner category, they are quite different. Routine takes a manual approach to daily planning tasks on to your calendar while Motion uses AI to auto-schedule tasks and even full projects onto your calendar.

We recommend Motion for busy professionals and teams, and Routine more for those who want to dabble with the idea of daily planning since they have a basic free tier.

Motion and Routine cater to different needs, making the choice between them quite straightforward.

Routine serves well as a daily planner for personal use and habit building while Motion, with its emphasis on functionality, caters to users with numerous tasks and constantly evolving schedules.

Routine, with its beautiful and minimal design, is ideal for personal use and habit formation. It allows for time-blocking tasks on your calendar but makes adjusting your schedule for sudden changes quite manual and tedious.

For instance, if you need to insert a new task between two existing ones, it requires a manual reshuffle of your calendar, which can be a bit cumbersome.

That said, Routine is great for anyone aiming to establish consistent habits—be it hydration, fitness routines, family commitments, or even weekly grocery shopping, complete with the ability to make notes, such as jotting down your grocery list for example.

Routine also has a 'free forever' tier, which adds to its appeal for personal task management without any financial commitment.

Join Routine Waiting List.

Motion, in contrast, is better suited for professionals with dynamic workloads.

Its AI feature prioritizes and rearranges tasks based on urgency. It also includes a meeting scheduler and project management tool, making it more aligned with professional use.

When you add a task in Motion, you can assign it a priority level (low, medium, high), and the app intelligently rearranges your schedule to accommodate this new task. For example, if you add a high-priority task amongst existing ones, Motion will automatically adjust your schedule, ensuring that your most critical tasks are addressed first. This dynamic scheduling is a game-changer for professionals with evolving workloads.

Get Motion 7 Day Free Trial.

In short, choose Routine for a structured approach to personal habits and tasks, and Motion for managing a changing professional workload with AI-assisted scheduling.

AI Auto-Scheduling
AI Auto-Scheduling
Best
Best

Motion is in a completely different league for AI auto-scheduling. It automatically builds and updates your day, reschedules everything if plans shift, and keeps your tasks prioritized without you lifting a finger. You never have to drag, drop, or reshuffle anything yourself, if something changes, Motion just handles it.

Routine doesn't do any of this. You're stuck manually moving every task, and when your plans change, it's all on you to rearrange your schedule. There's no auto-prioritizing or smart adjustment, so you can't trust it to keep your day on track.

If you want your planner to actually build and maintain your schedule for you, Motion is the only real option here. Routine isn't even close.

Adaptive Rescheduling
Adaptive Rescheduling
Best
Best

Motion completely takes the headache out of changing plans by automatically shuffling your whole schedule whenever something comes up. Meetings, tasks, deep work blocks, everything gets adjusted in real time so you never have to drag things around or stress about missing deadlines. Even if you miss a task or your team's plans shift, Motion updates everything instantly and flags any issues, so nothing falls through the cracks.

Routine, on the other hand, doesn't do any of this. If something changes or you have an urgent new task, you're stuck manually rearranging your schedule. It won't shift things for you, so if your day isn't perfectly static, it just creates more work and frustration.

If adaptive rescheduling matters to you, Motion is on a completely different level. Routine doesn't even try to handle it. Pick Motion and stop worrying about your schedule falling apart when plans change.

Cognitive Clarity
Cognitive Clarity
Best
Best

Motion takes all the mental juggling out of your day by automatically prioritizing, scheduling, and updating your plans as things change, so you always know what matters most without any effort. You never have to manually rework your schedule or wonder if you're missing something, because Motion does it for you and keeps your priorities front and center.

Routine, on the other hand, actually makes things messier. It gives you more ways to organize, but they just end up adding confusion and extra work. You still have to manually adjust everything when your plans change, and it's easy to get lost or even delete important stuff by accident.

If you want less mental clutter and a planner that truly keeps your head clear, Motion is the obvious pick. Routine just adds to the chaos.

Planning Methodology
Planning Methodology
Best
Best

Motion makes planning almost effortless. You just start using it and everything falls into place, calendar, tasks, meetings, priorities, all handled in a way that feels natural. The AI keeps your schedule running smoothly, so you're not wasting time shuffling things around or getting lost in menus. Even if the interface can get a bit cluttered, you always know what's happening next and don't have to fight with the system.

Routine, on the other hand, turns even simple planning into a struggle. You're left guessing how to do basic things, and it's easy to make mistakes that mess up your setup. The organization tools actually add confusion, and you have to manually clean up your schedule every time something changes. It might look clean, but using it isn't.

If you want a daily planner that's truly easy to pick up and keeps planning simple, Motion is the clear choice. Routine just gets in your way.

Integrations
Integrations
Draw

Both Motion and Routine are pretty limited on integrations, but for different reasons. Motion gives you a couple of native options (email and Siri) and recently added a basic API and Zapier integration, so you can at least start to automate project management tasks and hack together some connections. But it still has big holes, like no way to sync with appointment schedulers or custom fields, and you're stuck with manual work for most cross-app workflows.

Routine, on the other hand, only connects directly with Google Workspace and Notion. There's no API and no way to hook into anything else yet, so if you need your planner to pull from more than those two, you're out of luck. None of the big names like Slack or Asana are actually available yet.

If you need even basic flexibility or want to start piecing together integrations beyond just Google and Notion, Motion has a slight edge since you can at least use Zapier or their API for some workflows, even though it's still clunky. Routine is more locked down and really only fits if you live inside Google and Notion. Neither is great for serious centralization, but Motion is a bit less frustrating if you have to pick.

SavvyCal gives you a smoother, more personal scheduling experience with thoughtful touches like letting people drag to pick times, plus a cleaner interface. If you care most about making scheduling easy for your guests, especially for straightforward bookings, SavvyCal is the more pleasant tool.

Motion's scheduler is better if you want your meetings tightly linked to your tasks, projects, and actual work calendar. It's more about organizing your whole workflow than just booking meetings. Neither tool is built for deep, complex scheduling automation, but SavvyCal is clearly stronger for teams who just want a polished, guest-friendly scheduling flow. Pick Motion only if your main need is to have scheduling as a piece of a larger project management system. For pure scheduling, SavvyCal is the obvious choice.
