Editor's Verdict
Editor's Verdict
Main Category
Main Category
Copy Link

If you're still using Loom, I promise you there is a better path.

We were obsessed with Loom when it first came out, but the Atlassian acquisition destroyed it. It became super buggy and slow. It would crash and we'd need to re-start our filming 2-3 times just to capture it. It got bloated and more cluttered, honestly it was just frustrating and unreliable in our day-to-day, especially when we were trying to move quickly.

That's what pushed us to start looking for alternatives. We tried a handful of tools like Tella and Cap, but nothing really simplified our workflow or felt like a true upgrade. Then we came across Supercut, and we immediately absolutely love it (Read our full Supercut Review). It felt like what Loom used to be, but modern, faster, way more intentional, and most importantly, reliable.

Key Feature Comparisons
Recorder

Even just opening the recorder is night and day. Loom feels like everything is fighting for your attention. It takes multiple clicks before you ever start recording. With Supercut, it's just... clean. Everything is laid out for you; the UI is minimal, no clutter in sight, and no annoying upsells.

After you've recorded, Supercut keeps that seamless professional feel. With Loom, there's always a ton of pop-ups telling you to sign in, over and over again, but with Supercut, the sharing process is frictionless.

Playback & Transcripts

When someone is watching your video back in Loom, the overwhelm is real. The transcript takes up a huge portion of the screen, the call to record floats over the chapters, and the auto-generated summary takes up what's left of the screen space.

On Supercut, you just get a superior experience. Your video becomes the main focus with chapter, transcripts, comments displayed neatly on the right, and the AI assistant and reaction/comment tucked nicely below your video. Plus, everything loads quickly, and there's no lag in sight.

Transcripts view while watching a Supercut video

AI Features

Once you've recorded your video and go to watch it back, the difference becomes really clear. Supercuts AI assistant lets you "ask anything", pulling insights or even turning your video into usable documentation. The first time I realized it could generate full SOPs, I literally said, "Oh my god, this is so good!" out loud.

Looms AI doesn't feel nearly as intuitive, and it's locked behind yet another upgrade. We were constantly being prompted to upgrade tiers without ever really understanding what we were getting. Supercut keeps it simple: if you're on Pro, you have access to everything. That clarity makes it feel so much safer to actually build your workflows around.

Sales

Both Loom and Supercut let you add branding and calls to action, but if you're aiming for a more polished, professional experience overall, we recommend Supercut. It simply feels more refined from start to finish.

Final Thoughts

Supercut as a tool is great, and the video recording abilities got us excited, but it's what it enabled that made us fans. We were able to completely replace a huge chunk of our meetings, reduce back-and-forth with clients, and start documenting processes without thinking twice. It just became how we operate.

If you're trying to decide between the two, it's really a no brainer, go with Supercut (and don't forget to grab this Supercut discount).

Supercut stands out for async team collaboration and efficient screen recording in a work context. It's become the go-to for replacing meetings and handling internal or customer communication, with people preferring to get a Supercut over traditional calls or even other tools like Loom. ### Superhuman Mail vs Gmail
**Live Link**: [https://efficient.app/compare/superhuman-vs-gmail](https://efficient.app/compare/superhuman-vs-gmail)

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
Superhuman simply helps make checking your emails less dreadful and more enjoyable. Every action, including loading, is nearly instantaneous (unlike Gmail). We've used Superhuman for 6+ years and definitely think it's worth it compared to using Gmail.

Editor's Verdict
Editor's Verdict
Copy Link
Close All

If you're searching for Gmail alternatives, you're probably already using Gmail and are trying to figure out if paying for an email productivity tool like Superhuman is worth it. We'll make it easy and say yes—if you fit into the category below ⤵

Love or hate Gmail, Google has to consider billions of users with every single change that they roll out, this generally slows down their ability to innovate and build innovative features that the masses might not need. That said, when comparing Gmail vs Outlook (Microsoft 365), it is infinitely better, in our eyes. The biggest frustration though is that it's getting quite bloated with unnecessary widgets (e.g. now with the chat and everything there). In-fact, as an avid Gmail user for over a decade, I began finding that I have the thought to send an email, and by the time Gmail loads, I've totally forgotten what I was even about to do.

Enter Superhuman Mail. Superhuman is a premium email tool built for tech savvy folks who just want a better email experience, both on the web and on mobile. The beautiful minimal interface that loads exceptionally quick not only saves us time, but makes the entire email inbox experience much more manageable.

Purposeful Design
Purposeful Design
Best
Best

Superhuman Mail is built to keep you focused. Its minimal design and Inbox Splits let you jump straight to exactly the emails you care about, so you never get buried in a wall of distractions or lose track of what matters. Every part of the layout is stripped down to protect your attention, both on desktop and mobile, making it easy to triage quickly without your brain wandering off.

Gmail is the opposite here. The interface is cluttered, with colors, popups, and extra products like chat and meet fighting for your attention. Even after you try to organize things with labels, the sidebar gets messy and you're always dropped into your main inbox, so you have to dig around every time. Slow loading just makes it easier to get sidetracked and forget what you were doing.

If your priority is staying focused and powering through email without distractions, Superhuman Mail is miles ahead. Gmail just doesn't keep you on track the same way.

Speed & Productivity
Speed & Productivity
Best
Best

Superhuman Mail is built entirely around keyboard shortcuts and flow, so you barely touch the mouse and everything feels instant. Switching inbox views, snoozing, scheduling, replying in-line, and using snippets all happen with a single key or two and don't break your rhythm. Even advanced stuff like scheduling based on someone's habits or time zone, or sending "if no reply," is just part of the normal workflow. Offline mode works so smoothly you might not even notice you lost connection.

Gmail, on the other hand, has some basic shortcuts but they're inconsistent and awkward to use. Anything beyond the basics, like snoozing, scheduling, or templates, forces you back to hunting with your mouse and clicking through menus. Inline replies and templates are slow and clunky, with too many steps and hidden settings. Offline mode is forgettable unless you remembered to enable it beforehand.

If speed and productivity are what matter, Superhuman Mail absolutely runs circles around Gmail. If you want to get through your inbox fast and stay in the zone, pick Superhuman Mail.

AI Assistance
AI Assistance
Draw

Superhuman Mail makes a much bigger impact here. Its auto-labels are simple to set up, instantly show you results as you write prompts, and are easy to refine or rename later, all without leaving the main console. Plus, you can auto-archive by label to keep your inbox clean, which Gmail doesn't offer right now.

AI search in Superhuman is right in the search bar where you'd expect, so you'll actually use it. Gmail's Gemini search is strong but tucked away behind a separate button, so it's easy to forget it exists.

Most importantly, Superhuman's AI writes emails that actually sound like you, because it learns your tone from all your emails and just works, no setup needed. Gmail's AI writing is robotic and awkward, often creating more work by tacking on new drafts instead of improving your original message. It can't match your style and doesn't save you as much time.

If you want AI that really nails both writing and organizing emails with minimal hassle, Superhuman Mail is the clear pick. Gmail might catch up when its new Gemini features roll out, but right now, Superhuman is way ahead.

Follow-Up
Follow-Up
Best
Best

Superhuman Mail is in a completely different league for follow-up. You get instant, built-in read receipts that actually work and show exactly when and how often your message gets opened, which makes it way easier to know when to ping someone or just chill. On top of that, Superhuman drafts follow-up replies for you in your own tone using AI, so you barely have to do anything except review and send. They're even pushing this further so it can handle replies for basically any email by using your connected docs and previous emails.

Gmail, meanwhile, is stuck on awkward read receipt requests that you have to send to the other person, which just feels clunky and weird. There's nothing to help you actually follow up, so you're left doing everything manually.

If you want smart, effortless follow-up, Superhuman Mail crushes Gmail. No contest.

Team Collaboration
Team Collaboration
Best
Best

Superhuman Mail absolutely wipes the floor with Gmail when it comes to team collaboration. Gmail just doesn't have any team collaboration features at all, so if you need to comment, assign, or share within your email, it's a nonstarter.

Superhuman Mail, on the other hand, actually lets you comment with your team, share drafts, and even @mention people outside your team for input, which is a lifesaver when you're working with contractors. The extra touches like Team Read Receipts and Team Reply Indicators mean you're never left guessing who's handling what or whether a message's been seen, which really cuts down on wasted time.

If collaborating on email is even slightly important to you or your team, Superhuman Mail is the only real choice here. Gmail just isn't built for this.

Purposeful Design
Purposeful Design
Best

Superhuman Mail is built for people who want to get in, triage fast, and stay focused. Its ultra-minimal design, lack of clutter, and customizable Inbox Splits mean you can always see exactly what matters and move through your emails without getting pulled off track. If you want to triage your inbox quickly and keep your head clear, Superhuman Mail is in a completely different league. Hey just isn't designed for anyone who needs to process email with purpose.

Speed & Productivity
Speed & Productivity
Best
Best

Superhuman Mail blows past Hey when it comes to speed and productivity. Everything in Superhuman Mail is designed to keep you moving fast: it's keyboard-first, lets you switch inbox views instantly, reply in-line with a keystroke, and schedule or snooze emails in seconds. Features like Smart Send, natural language scheduling, and instant snippets mean you stay in flow and never have to touch the mouse if you don't want to. Even offline, Superhuman Mail doesn't slow you down.

Hey actually gets in your way if you care about moving quickly. The interface is clunky, there are no real keyboard shortcuts or productivity boosts, and you have to do everything manually. Instead of speeding up your workflow, Hey forces you to adapt to its opinionated structure, which just adds friction if you're trying to be efficient.

If you want to fly through your inbox and care about speed or productivity, Superhuman Mail is the clear pick. Hey only makes sense if you want things simple and don't mind slowing down.

AI Assistance
AI Assistance
Best
Best

Superhuman Mail is on a completely different level than Hey when it comes to AI assistance. Superhuman actually writes your emails in your own tone, organizes your inbox with auto-labels you can easily refine, and answers natural language questions about your messages right from the search bar. It saves you real time by handling drafts and sorting without any manual setup.

Hey doesn't offer any AI help at all. You're stuck doing all the writing and organizing yourself, and the inbox structure is rigid with only basic manual screening. If you want your email to save you time by handling the busywork and sounding like you, Superhuman Mail is the only real choice here.

Follow-Up
Follow-Up
Best
Best

Superhuman Mail dominates Hey when it comes to follow-up. Superhuman gives you instant read receipts so you know exactly when your emails are opened, making it easy to time your follow-ups or just chill knowing your message was seen. On top of that, it auto-drafts follow-up replies in your own tone using AI, so you barely have to think about what to say next, just review and hit send.

Hey leaves you stranded. There are no smart follow-up features at all. No reminders, no tracking, no automation, nothing. You're stuck remembering everything yourself, which means you'll move slower and definitely miss some opportunities.

If follow-up is even a little bit important to you, go for Superhuman Mail.

Team Collaboration
Team Collaboration
Best
Best

Superhuman Mail is miles ahead for team collaboration. You can comment, share drafts, @mention anyone (even non-users), and track read receipts and reply status across your team. This means no more duplicate replies or chasing down teammates for status updates, and it's a huge boost if you work with contractors or rely on email for team workflows.

Hey doesn't let you comment, assign, or share emails at all. You're stuck doing everything outside the app, which means extra steps and constant context-switching.

If collaborating with your team over email matters even a little, Superhuman Mail is the way to go.

Purposeful Design
Purposeful Design
Best
Best

Superhuman Mail is built around keeping you focused. Its minimal design cuts out all distractions, and the Inbox Splits feature means you're never looking at a giant mess of emails. You jump straight to what matters, whether that's team messages, customer issues, or accounting, and handle each group without getting sidetracked. Even on mobile, you get the same clear, distraction-free setup.

Missive sticks to a classic email client look. If you like the familiar Apple Mail or Outlook layout, you'll feel at home, and it does a nice job of pulling in chats and messages from other channels so you're not bouncing between apps. But it doesn't do anything special to help you focus or avoid overwhelm, it just keeps things tidy and unified.

If you want to triage email as quickly as possible without getting distracted, Superhuman Mail is the clear pick. It's the only one that actively protects your attention and keeps your workflow laser-focused. Missive is solid and comfortable, especially if you're used to traditional email, but it doesn't go out of its way to help you stay on task.

Speed & Productivity
Speed & Productivity
Best
Best

Superhuman Mail leaves Missive in the dust when it comes to speed and productivity. Everything in Superhuman is built around keyboard shortcuts and staying in your flow, from switching inboxes to scheduling, snoozing, and replying inline. You can do things like Smart Send, reply to specific text in seconds, and pop out compose windows with a single shortcut, all without losing momentum. Even offline, Superhuman keeps you moving with fast search and full email controls.

Missive does help you triage across multiple channels and makes team delegation fast, but the interface gets cluttered and the extra features actually slow you down. The AI tools require setup and don't just work out of the box. If you're focused on pure speed and never breaking stride in your inbox, Superhuman is the clear pick. Missive is fine if you need to wrangle a lot of team channels, but it just can't keep up with Superhuman's relentless focus on flow and keyboard-driven productivity.

AI Assistance
AI Assistance
Draw

Superhuman Mail stands out for AI Assistance because everything works out of the box, with no setup or extra costs. Its AI actually learns your tone from all your emails, so the replies sound like you wrote them, and it keeps getting better without you doing anything. Auto-labeling and search are baked into the workflow and super easy to use, so you waste less time organizing or finding emails.

Missive can get you similar AI help, but you have to set up your own AI model and pay for it separately. Once you do, it's strong at pulling in context and generating relevant replies, especially for teams with complex inboxes, but it's not nearly as simple or automatic as Superhuman. You'll save time with Missive if you invest in the setup, but Superhuman is just way smoother and more user friendly.

If you want AI that writes and organizes emails like you would and saves you the most time with zero hassle, Superhuman Mail is the clear pick. Only go with Missive if you need custom AI models and are willing to handle the setup yourself.

Follow-Up
Follow-Up
Best
Best

Superhuman Mail crushes it on follow-up. You get instant read receipts so you know exactly when your email was opened and how often, which makes it way easier to time your next move or just chill if you know they're mulling it over. Plus, the auto-draft feature actually writes your follow-ups in your style, so you barely have to do anything except review and send.

Missive gives you nothing here. No reminders, no prompts, no smart tools, you're stuck remembering everything yourself, which means stuff will slip through the cracks.

If you care about not missing opportunities and want follow-ups to be fast and basically handled for you, Superhuman Mail is the obvious pick. Missive just can't compete on this.

Team Collaboration
Team Collaboration
Draw

Missive is built from the ground up for team collaboration, with commenting, assigning, and sharing baked into every part of the workflow. Everything happens inside the inbox, comment threads, @mentions, and assignments are seamless, and you never need to leave the context of the conversation or juggle other tools. It's made so teams can triage and act together without friction, even across email, SMS, and social channels. If your team lives in a shared inbox and needs to hand off, discuss, and track everything in one place, Missive just makes the headaches disappear.

Superhuman Mail brings strong collaboration tools too, especially with the ability to @mention both team members and outsiders in comments, which is a unique plus for teams working with contractors. Shared drafts, Team Read Receipts, and Team Reply Indicators make coordination smoother and help teams avoid double work. If you rely on knowing who's seen or is replying to what, or you need to loop in non-users, Superhuman Mail nails those details.

But if pure team collaboration is your main concern, commenting, assigning, and sharing without friction, Missive is the clear pick. It's purpose-built for teams handling shared inboxes and constant messaging, and it removes all the usual pain points. Superhuman Mail is helpful for teams who need visibility and external collaboration, but it can't match how deeply Missive is optimized for seamless teamwork inside the inbox.

Notion Failed You

Notion Failed You
Watch
113.0K views
Editor's Verdict
Editor's Verdict
Copy Link
Close All

While both Notion Mail and Superhuman Mail provide a better user experience than Gmail, Superhuman Mail goes above and beyond with its features for busy professionals looking to get through their inbox faster, while Notion's price point may be better suited for students or those just looking for a better user interface.

I've been using Superhuman Mail for 6+ years, so when Notion Mail came out, I was curious to give it a shot. I connected my inbox and tried to use it as my daily email app, but within a day I realized it was nowhere near as thought out as Superhuman Mail.

With Superhuman Mail, I can triage my entire inbox incredibly quickly: snooze emails, schedule replies, mute threads, archive messages, and jump between Split Inboxes without touching my mouse. With Notion Mail, I kept breaking flow because I had to pause, reach for my mouse, as the features weren't all built to be keyboard first. It looked nice, but it slowed me down. The AI features were no where near Superhuman Mail's and at that point, I knew it couldn't be my main email client.

Notion and Superhuman both offer the ability to split similar types of emails into different "views" (as Notion calls them) or Split Inboxes (as Superhuman Mail calls them).

Essentially, with both tools, you can either use AI to auto-categorize emails or manually set up a filter yourself that helps segment your inbox. This helps you triage through "like" emails at the same time, aiding with context switching and saving time.

Superhuman Mail takes this one step further with its workflows feature. You can set up rules for how you'd like emails to be handled (e.g., use AI to auto-respond to a job application, auto-forward to your hiring manager, and mark as done in your inbox, all without you touching it).

They hardly compare in terms of their AI email assistant features. Notion Mail's AI is similar to what you'd get in Gmail, while Superhuman Mail actually drafts email responses in your tone. YES, in your voice. It learns from your previous messages and responds as you would. AND it drafts emails for you automatically. An email lands in your inbox, and Superhuman Mail writes the reply. All you need to do is review it and send.

Second, Superhuman Mail even figures out if an email you sent needs a follow-up/nudge, and it automatically drafts a follow-up in your voice and leaves it in your inbox for you to hit send (just like an executive assistant would 🤯).

Third, Superhuman Mail uses AI to search your inbox. Forget trying to find the perfect search term to surface an email. With Superhuman Mail, you can ask any question in plain language, and it will give you an AI summary response (e.g., "Where was the conversation left off with XYZ? When is the last time I spoke to XYZ?").

The one advantage Notion Mail has is its integration with Notion Pages. You can use Notion AI to reference a Notion Page, and it will draft a response based on the context of the page. That said, you'd have to be a very heavy Notion user for this to offer an advantage.

Superhuman Mail is hands down the better email app. It also has a ton of other features which you can read about in our full Superhuman Mail Review (like the ability to share and collaborate on emails with your team). Notion Mail may only provide advantages if you're a super hardcore Notion user or just want a better Gmail interface.

If you're on the fence, would highly recommend at least trying Superhuman Mail for free for 30 days (Superhuman Mail discount) so you can truly experience the power of the tool (although, we'll warn you that you might never want to go back to using anything else for email!).

Purposeful Design
Purposeful Design
Draw

Superhuman Mail stands out because its entire design is built to keep you focused and moving fast. The minimal interface means no distractions, and the Inbox Splits system breaks your email into clear, purpose-driven groups. You always know where to look and can jump straight to what matters, which means you're never bogged down by an endless feed or forced to scan through irrelevant messages.

Notion Mail does try to reduce clutter compared to Gmail, but its triage experience is slower and more awkward. You have to stop and manually click into each view, and there's no quick navigation between filters. The lack of keyboard shortcuts and the cramped compose window add friction, making it easy to lose momentum or get sidetracked.

If you care about staying on task and want the fastest, most focused triage, Superhuman Mail is the clear pick. Notion Mail is a cleaner Gmail, but it can't match Superhuman's deep-work, distraction-proof design.

Speed & Productivity
Speed & Productivity
Draw

Superhuman Mail is built entirely around speed, letting you stay in flow with true keyboard-first navigation. You can switch inbox splits, snooze, schedule, and even pop out compose screens without touching your mouse. Quick replies and in-line quoting take seconds and never break your momentum. Their snippet handling is lightning fast and even gives you analytics, and the offline mode is so seamless you barely notice if you lose internet.

Notion Mail has some handy shortcuts and preset snippets, but you'll still find yourself reaching for the mouse for basic actions like changing inbox views or using snooze and send later. In-line replies are a hassle, requiring multiple manual steps, and the offline mode is limited to just reading and composing on desktop.

If speed and pure productivity matter most, Superhuman Mail blows Notion Mail out of the water. The difference is night and day for anyone who wants to move fast and avoid friction. Only pick Notion Mail here if you're already deep into their ecosystem and don't mind trading some speed for that integration. Otherwise, Superhuman Mail is the obvious choice.

AI Assistance
AI Assistance
Best
Best

Superhuman Mail stands way ahead when it comes to AI Assistance. Its auto-labeling is not only simple to set up but also lets you actively see results as you write and refine prompts, making adjustments a breeze. If you want to tweak a label or prompt, you can do it right in the same console without starting over. Plus, Superhuman goes further by letting you auto-archive labels to keep your inbox clutter-free.

On top of that, Superhuman's AI Search is built right into the search bar and answers natural language questions about your inbox, referencing the actual emails it pulls from. Most impressively, Superhuman writes emails in your own tone by learning from all your previous messages, and it needs almost no human tweaking after the first draft.

Notion Mail, on the other hand, feels clunky by comparison. You can create auto-labels with a prompt, but if you want to change the criteria or label name later, you have to redo the whole thing from scratch. There's no AI search or summarization, and the AI drafting doesn't capture your tone or feedback. The only real plus is if you're deep in the Notion ecosystem and need to pull in Notion pages while emailing, but even then, the AI assistance itself just isn't up to par.

If you actually want your email app to save you time and sound like you, Superhuman is the clear choice. Notion Mail only makes sense if referencing Notion pages is absolutely essential for you, but for real AI help, it just doesn't compete.

Follow-Up
Follow-Up
Best
Best

Superhuman Mail absolutely crushes follow-up. You get instant read receipts, so you always know if your email landed and when to nudge again. The AI auto-drafts personalized replies in your own tone, making follow-ups almost effortless. You barely have to think about timing or what to say next.

Notion Mail doesn't even show if your email was opened and offers nothing for follow-up. You're left guessing every time.

If following up matters at all, Superhuman Mail is the only real option here. Notion Mail just leaves you in the dark.

Team Collaboration
Team Collaboration
Best
Best

Superhuman Mail is built for teams who need to comment, assign, and share directly in their inbox. You can @mention both team members and even outside collaborators for comments, track who's read what, and see when a teammate is replying so you don't step on each other's toes. It's the kind of setup that saves a ton of back-and-forth and is ideal if your team relies on email to get work done.

Notion Mail doesn't have any team collaboration features at all, so it's not even in the running for this. If you care about working together in email, Superhuman Mail is the only real choice here.

Are you working for a larger company that's locked into Microsoft 365 and have at-least a handful of emails that come in on a day-to-day basis? If so, Superhuman is sure to make this experience just a tad bit more enjoyable, bet of yet it integrates directly with Outlook so it'll work out of the box.

Enter Superhuman. Superhuman is a premium email tool built for tech savvy folks who just want a better email experience, both on the web and on mobile. The beautiful minimal interface that loads exceptionally quick not only saves us time, but makes the entire email inbox experience much more manageable.

Try a free month of Superhuman

Purposeful Design
Purposeful Design

Superhuman Mail is built so you can triage emails fast without ever getting sidetracked. Its whole setup revolves around Inbox Splits, so your emails are sorted into focused mini inboxes by rules you set. There's zero clutter, no distractions, and it's just as clean on mobile. You always know exactly where to focus, whether you have two minutes or twenty.

Outlook doesn't offer any evidence of purposeful design for focused triage. There's nothing in the summary that suggests it helps you avoid distractions or sort through emails quickly.

If you care about getting through your inbox without losing focus, Superhuman Mail is the clear choice. Outlook just can't compete here.

Speed & Productivity

Best
Best

Superhuman Mail is built for speed and nonstop flow. You can reply inline in seconds, pop out compose windows without losing context, and do all of this even offline without missing a beat.

Outlook, on the other hand, doesn't have any specific strengths called out for speed or productivity based on the review content. There's no mention of keyboard-first workflow, fast replies, or any of the advanced features that keep things moving.

If you care about flying through your inbox and keeping your momentum, Superhuman Mail is the clear pick here. Outlook just doesn't compete on this front.

AI Assistance

Superhuman Mail is miles ahead when it comes to AI Assistance. Its auto-labeling is not just easy to set up, but actively pulls in emails as you write prompts, and you can tweak everything right in the same place. You also get auto-archiving to keep your inbox clean without effort. The AI search is right in the search bar, so you actually use it, and it answers questions in plain language with references to the source emails.

But what really sets Superhuman apart is how its AI writes emails that actually sound like you, because it learns your tone from all your past messages. There's no setup, no fiddling, just natural, personable email drafts with hardly any human corrections needed.

Outlook doesn't have a summary for this criterion, so there's no evidence it does anything comparable. If saving time and having AI actually handle your email writing and organizing for you matters, Superhuman Mail is the clear pick.

Follow-Up
Follow-Up
Best
Best

Superhuman Mail blows Outlook out of the water on follow-up. Superhuman gives you instant read receipts, so you always know when your emails are opened and how often, making it way easier to time your follow-ups or just chill if you see someone is still thinking about your message.

On top of that, Superhuman drafts follow-up replies for you using AI in your own tone, so all you have to do is check it and send. They're pushing this even further by working toward auto-drafting for any email that needs a response, pulling in info from your previous emails and even your docs or wiki.

Outlook doesn't mention any follow-up support at all. If following up quickly and not missing opportunities matters to you, Superhuman Mail is the clear choice.

Team Collaboration
Team Collaboration
Best
Best

Superhuman Mail is on a completely different level for team collaboration. You can comment, share drafts, @mention teammates and even loop in people outside your team right inside the email thread. Features like Team Read Receipts and Team Reply Indicators actually prevent wasted work and make it clear who's handling what, which is huge if your team deals with high email volume or contractors.

Outlook doesn't have any of this in the review. There's no mention of commenting, assigning, or sharing features for teams.

If you care about collaborating smoothly with your team over email, Superhuman Mail is the only real option here. Outlook just doesn't show up for this.

### Superhuman Mail vs Spark
**Live Link**: [https://efficient.app/compare/superhuman-vs-spark](https://efficient.app/compare/superhuman-vs-spark)

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
Most people compare Spark to Superhuman and want to hear that Spark is better because there is a free tier... but we're here to say that Superhuman is far superior and worth the investment (we've been paying customers for 6+ years!).

We do think that Spark is better than Gmail/Outlook and is a good option if you're an individual on a budget, but if a professional and you're looking for "the best" email experience, Superhuman takes the cake.

Best Email Client: $0 vs $400 Email

Editor's Verdict
Editor's Verdict
Copy Link
Close All

When comparing Spark vs Superhuman, most folks fixate on the price difference. Spark has a free version (or is $60 per year on the paid tier), while Superhuman comes in at $30 per month, so you may be wondering, is Superhuman really worth the extra cost?

Well, after using and being paying customers of Superhuman for 6+ years, we're here to tell you that yes, it is 😄

The truth of the matter is that while Spark and Superhuman seem to have similar features on the surface, Superhuman goes the extra mile and puts extra TLC into each feature. Plus Superhuman is built more for professional use, while Spark is something that just any who wants a better email experience than Gmail or Outlook could use.

Here are some examples of how Spark and Superhuman differ ⤵️

While Spark's interface is miles better than Gmail or Outlook, it still feels—well—like email. Superhuman on the other hand gives your entire email app a facelift with its beautiful minimal design, consistent colors, fonts, and automatic resizing of emails to make them readable no matter your screen size. While Spark tries to do a bit of this, it's clear that less TLC went into the interface. For instance, in Spark, emails stretch with your screen, making them more difficult to read.

Spark email interface

In Spark, emails stretch with your screen, making them more difficult to read.

Superhuman sizes your emails perfectly no matter your screen size. Also, no bright colors, and just a beautiful minimal email view. Also take note of the AI summary of the email up top! This automatically happens with each email.

With Spark, you can snooze emails, but the ability to write in natural language is nowhere near Superhuman's. For instance, in Superhuman, you can snooze until "Next Wednesday at 9 a.m. in Australia". Superhuman automatically does the time conversion for you and allows you to fully write in natural language, while with Spark, you really can only choose from the predetermined options like tomorrow, or a specific date.

We use natural language multiple times a day when snoozing our emails in Superhuman and can't imagine having guardrails with limited options.

Spark + AI and Superhuman AI helps you summarize and respond to email messages faster.

With Superhuman, we see it going just one level deeper, training the AI to "write in your tone" based on your previous emails. While we don't use AI a ton for writing emails, the "write in my tone" feature in Superhuman is the one we tend to lean on the most.

It also seems that Superhuman takes in far more context from the entire conversation than Spark does, but that also makes sense because there is a cost associated with this, and since Superhuman is a premium product, they can spend more on AI tokens (giving the prompt more context).

You can summarize emails with Spark + AI on the paid tier. Superhuman does this AI summary automatically for you.

Simply write a prompt and allow Spark + AI to generate a response. You can ask it to change the tone of your email, or shorten/expand it.

Spark compose window

Spark Email AI Response With Prompt

With Superhuman, write down your thoughts and ask it to "rewrite in your voice" which is learns from previous emails 🤯. I think it's important to still sound "like you" in emails, and I found that Superhuman even pulls out emoji's that I typically use when using this feature!

While Spark Mail categorizes your emails based on people, newsletters, and notifications, Superhuman allows you to go deeper and categorize emails based on your own preferences into Split Inbox (e.g. emails with a similar subject type, or senders from a specific domain). We use the Split Inbox feature in Superhuman to help us triage similar emails together, reducing the need to context switch.

Spark email inbox listview

Notifications and Newsletters are automatically grouped together in Spark, but adding further customization isn't possible.

Superhuman beautifully splits your inbox for you based on how you customize it.

Spark has a unified inbox, meaning that all of your emails can technically come into one inbox and some people tend to *love* this. It can be helpful if you have multiple work emails for example, and you want them all to come into one inbox, but you still want to maintain the separate emails.

With Superhuman, while you don't have a unified inbox, you can simply jump to another inbox using the keyboard shortcut CTRL 1, 2, 3, etc. On the Superhuman mobile app, swiping through accounts is as easy as clicking your profile icon.

We actually prefer having the inboxes separated as mentally, checking work emails vs personal emails require different mental effort, so even if Superhuman offered this feature, we'd have to think twice before turning it on 🤔.

Overall, if you're a professional who wants the best email client, claiming the Superhuman Mail free trial is probably worth it alongside Spark, to really compare the two tools and see which one you get the most out of.

For us, when it comes to emails for work and improving efficiency, there is a clear winner of the two, with Superhuman taking the cake.

Purposeful Design
Purposeful Design
Best
Best

Superhuman Mail stands out because its whole setup is built to keep you locked in and focused, with zero clutter and inbox splits you control. You always know exactly where to look, so you never waste time or get overwhelmed by a flood of emails. Everything is stripped down to just what matters, both on desktop and mobile, so you can triage fast without distractions.

Spark tries to help with focus, but still has clutter in the compose screen and spreads emails out in a way that makes reading tiring. Its auto-sorting helps a bit, but since you can't customize those categories, most of your emails end up lumped together and you still have to dig through everything.

If you care about staying focused and getting through email quickly, Superhuman Mail is on another level. Spark is fine, but you'll still run into distractions and wasted time. Go with Superhuman Mail if purposeful design matters to you.

Speed & Productivity
Speed & Productivity
Draw

Superhuman Mail is built around pure speed and flow. Every action is optimized for keyboard use, whether you're switching inbox splits, snoozing, scheduling, or replying inline. Natural language scheduling is a huge time saver, so you don't have to count out days or fiddle with specific dates. Inline replies are instant, just highlight and hit enter, so you never lose momentum. Snippets are lightning fast and even come with analytics, which is a rare bonus if you rely on canned responses. Offline mode is so smooth you probably won't even notice when you lose internet.

Spark tries to keep up with keyboard shortcuts and lets you navigate most features without a mouse, but it stumbles on the details. Custom snoozes force you to count days instead of just typing what you want, which slows you down. Inline replying is a pain, you have to copy, format, and quote manually, breaking your flow every time. Templates are accessible by shortcut, but they don't go as far as Superhuman's snippet analytics. Offline, Spark only caches recent emails and users say it's slow.

If speed and productivity are your top priorities, especially for heavy keyboard users, Superhuman Mail is on a different level. Spark covers the basics but feels clunky where it matters most. Go with Superhuman Mail if you want to move fast and stay in the zone.

AI Assistance
AI Assistance
Best
Best

Superhuman Mail absolutely outclasses Spark on AI Assistance. Superhuman's AI writes email drafts in your own tone by learning from all your past emails, not just a few. It handles replies automatically, catches when you need to follow up, and organizes your inbox with custom auto-labels you can tweak on the fly. The AI search actually lets you ask questions about your inbox in plain language and shows exactly where it found the info.

Spark, on the other hand, only learns from three emails, so its tone-matching is shallow. It auto-categorizes some emails but you can't set up your own labels, and there's no way to ask questions about your inbox, just basic summaries and translations. The AI help here is pretty limited and feels generic.

If you want the AI to really save you time and sound like you, Superhuman Mail is several leagues ahead. Spark is fine if you just want basic summaries, but it's nowhere close for true AI assistance.

Follow-Up
Follow-Up
Best
Best

Superhuman Mail is in a completely different league for follow-up. It gives you instant, built-in read receipts so you always know when someone's opened your email and how many times, making it easy to time your follow-ups perfectly or just chill when you know they've seen it.

On top of that, Superhuman auto-drafts follow-up replies in your own tone using AI, so all you have to do is review and send. They're even pushing this further by working towards drafting replies for any email that needs a response, pulling context from your previous messages and connected docs.

Spark only offers read receipts if you pay, and doesn't help with follow-up at all. If following up is important to you, there's no contest, Superhuman is the only real option here.

Team Collaboration
Team Collaboration
Draw

Spark edges out Superhuman Mail for pure team collaboration because it lets your team collaborate on email drafts in real-time and assign emails directly, making it easier to coordinate who responds and work together on messaging without any lag.

Superhuman Mail gets close with team comments, shared drafts, and unique touches like @mentioning non-users in comments, which is great for pulling in contractors. It also has Team Read Receipts and Reply Indicators, so you can see if someone's handling a thread or if a reply is coming, which stops you from duplicating work. If your workflow depends on keeping tabs on who's replying or if you need to loop in outside folks for comments, Superhuman Mail has a slight edge there.

But if you care most about seamless, real-time drafting and clear assignment within your team, Spark is just a bit more efficient and direct. Go with Spark if your team wants the fastest, most interactive collaboration on emails. Pick Superhuman Mail if your team relies on detailed visibility and often works with contractors who aren't on your platform. Both are strong, but Spark is a little smoother for core team workflows.

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
Superhuman Mail moves through email way faster and is actively supported, while Tatem is minimal but no longer being developed.

Only use Tatem if you absolutely have to, but pick Superhuman Mail for a much smoother and more reliable experience.

Purposeful Design
Purposeful Design
Draw

Superhuman Mail is built to keep you laser focused. Its whole triage setup is about segmenting your inbox into custom mini inboxes, so you always know where to focus and what can wait. There's zero clutter, and the design stays out of your way whether you're at your desk or on your phone. The review calls it the only tool that actually protects your attention, which says a lot if staying on task is your top priority.

Tatem also gives you a clean, distraction-free inbox. It's stripped down, loads fast, and lets you process emails quickly without getting pulled off track. Inbox splits and keyboard shortcuts help you batch and move faster, but you don't get much control or customization beyond the basics. If your needs are simple, Tatem keeps you moving, but you'll hit limits if you want to fine-tune how you triage.

If you care most about never getting sidetracked and want real control over where your focus goes, Superhuman Mail is the clear choice. Tatem will work if you just want minimalism and speed, but Superhuman Mail is on another level for purposeful triage.

Speed & Productivity
Speed & Productivity
Best
Best

Superhuman Mail absolutely flies when it comes to speed and productivity. Everything is built around keyboard shortcuts, and you can do just about anything without touching the mouse. Switching inbox views, snoozing, scheduling with natural language, smart send times, and even advanced snippet analytics all happen instantly and keep you in the zone. The inline reply flow is slick and saves real time, and offline mode works so well you barely notice if you lose connection.

Tatem is fast and distraction-free, with a clean interface that helps you move through basic email actions quickly. You get keyboard shortcuts, but they're simple and don't offer much depth. There's no advanced quick-reply or automation, so you're getting a bare-bones experience designed for straightforward needs.

If you just want something minimalist and quick for basic email, Tatem does the job. But if speed and productivity are your top priorities, Superhuman Mail isn't just faster, it's on another level with thoughtful features that keep you moving and never break your flow. No contest here: Superhuman Mail is the one to pick if you care about speed and getting more done.

AI Assistance
AI Assistance
Best
Best

Superhuman Mail absolutely runs circles around Tatem when it comes to AI Assistance. Superhuman writes emails in your own tone without setup, organizes everything with auto-labels you can tweak on the fly, and even auto-archives clutter so you barely have to touch your inbox. You just prompt and it handles the rest, saving you serious time and effort.

Tatem, on the other hand, doesn't do any of this. No AI help writing or sorting. You're left drafting and organizing every email by hand, so you miss out on all the time savings and smart automation that Superhuman pulls off.

If you want your email app to actually save you time and handle the busywork, Superhuman is the only real choice here. Tatem just isn't built for it.

Follow-Up
Follow-Up
Best
Best

Superhuman Mail absolutely crushes Tatem when it comes to follow-up. With Superhuman, you get instant read receipts so you know exactly when someone opens your email, plus AI-powered auto-drafts that actually write your follow-up replies in your own tone. That means you barely need to think about tracking or remembering who to nudge next.

Tatem, on the other hand, doesn't offer any smart follow-up features at all. You're stuck remembering everything yourself, which makes it way too easy to drop the ball or miss a reply.

If you care about not missing opportunities and want follow-ups handled fast and intelligently, Superhuman Mail is the only real choice here. Tatem just can't keep up.

Team Collaboration
Team Collaboration
Best
Best

If you care about team collaboration in your inbox, Superhuman Mail completely outclasses Tatem here. Superhuman Mail lets you comment, share drafts, and even pull in people outside your team for feedback, which is a lifesaver when working with contractors or anyone not already in your system. You get real-time visibility into who's read what and who's replying, so you don't step on each other's toes or waste time chasing updates. It even ties in directly with popular CRMs.

Tatem, on the other hand, gives you nothing for collaboration. You're stuck doing everything manually outside the app, which makes it a non-starter for any workflow where you need to work with others in your inbox.

If you need to collaborate with a team, Superhuman Mail is the only real option between these two. There's no contest.

Superhuman Suite brings together four standout tools under one subscription, while Microsoft 365 feels clunky and locks you into its ecosystem.

Superhuman Suite actually feels built for the way modern teams want to work, since it brings together tools like Coda, Grammarly, and a genuinely fast email client, all while letting you keep your core storage and email with Google or Microsoft. That means you're not locked into a single ecosystem and can mix and match, which is a huge deal if you like flexibility and using the best tool for each job.

Microsoft 365, on the other hand, really locks you into its own world and doesn't play well with outside tools. If you're used to the flexibility of Google Workspace or want your team to actually enjoy the apps they use every day, Microsoft 365 will just feel clunky and outdated.

If you care about working fast, using modern tools, and not getting boxed in, Superhuman Suite is the clear pick. Microsoft 365 only makes sense if you're already deep in the Microsoft ecosystem and don't mind giving up flexibility. For everyone else, Superhuman Suite is just way more enjoyable and adaptable.

Superlist stands out for its beautiful design, satisfying interactions, and features that make completing tasks genuinely enjoyable, but it's still held back by bugs and unreliable collaboration. Even basic personal use can get frustrating if you need to refresh the app just to keep things working, so it's not smooth sailing yet. If you care about aesthetics and a delightful experience, you'll love the attention to detail, but be ready for some hiccups, especially if you want to share tasks or notes with others.

TickTick is the opposite: plain, dated, but steady. It's simple and just works for basic personal lists and notes, without any real polish or modern feel. If you only care about jotting things down and don't need anything fancy, TickTick gets the job done with fewer surprises.

If you want a gorgeous, thoughtfully designed task manager and can tolerate occasional bugs, pick Superlist. If you just want something that's stable, straightforward, and free from distractions, even if it feels old, go with TickTick. For anyone who values reliability over looks, TickTick is the safer bet right now. If you're drawn to design and don't mind some rough edges, Superlist is the more exciting choice.

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
Superlist feels fun to use with its thoughtful design and satisfying noises, but it's buggy and collaboration is unreliable, while Todoist works reliably for personal tasks and does exactly what you expect.

Only use Superlist if you really care about design quirks, but stick with Todoist if you want something practical that just works.

If you care about design and a delightful personal task experience, Superlist blows Todoist out of the water. The UI feels fresh and satisfying, and even checking off tasks is fun thanks to the little noises and swiggly lines. But if you want something stable and reliable, especially for collaboration or anything beyond solo use, Superlist just isn't there yet. It's buggy, and even basic collaboration can break down, which gets frustrating fast.

Todoist, on the other hand, is steady and does exactly what you expect for personal task management. No surprises, but also no headaches. If you just want to get things done and not worry about glitches or unfinished team features, Todoist is the safer pick.

So if you're after a beautiful, playful solo task app and can tolerate a few bugs, try Superlist. If you want something that just works, especially for personal use, go with Todoist. For collaboration, neither is a home run right now, but Todoist is less likely to leave you annoyed.

### Surfe vs Whalesync
**Live Link**: [https://efficient.app/compare/surfe-vs-whalesync](https://efficient.app/compare/surfe-vs-whalesync)

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
Surfe lets you work with LinkedIn leads and CRM data together right inside LinkedIn, while Whalesync focuses on syncing data between apps like Webflow, Airtable, Notion, and HubSpot for complex connections.

Only use Whalesync if you need reliable 2-way sync across those platforms, but stick with Surfe if you mainly manage LinkedIn leads and CRM data.

1
Surfe
Recommended
Recommended

Best for syncing LinkedIn contacts to your CRM

Best for syncing LinkedIn contacts to your CRM
Try Surfe
2
Whalesync
Recommended
Recommended

Trending Right Now
Trending Right Now
Copy Link
1
Kick

Best AI accounting for small businesses

Best AI accounting for small businesses
Get 25% off
Editor's Verdict
Editor's Verdict
Copy Link

Surfe is unmatched if your focus is integrating LinkedIn with your CRM. It's built specifically for LinkedIn, so you get a seamless experience adding leads and syncing conversations directly inside LinkedIn itself. This targeted approach means everything feels smoother and more natural for anyone who spends their time building lead lists and managing contacts through LinkedIn. The only real downside is you can't add new leads from mobile, so you'll have to wait until you're back at your computer.

Whalesync, on the other hand, is all about true 2-way data sync between apps like Webflow, Airtable, Notion, and HubSpot. If you need complex, bidirectional syncing across these platforms, it's the clear choice. But it's not built for LinkedIn or CRM lead management, and it has a big weakness with rich text and blog post fields, where it can overwrite content and cause data loss.

If you're managing LinkedIn leads and want the deepest, smoothest CRM integration, Surfe is the obvious pick. If your world revolves around syncing data between Webflow, Airtable, Notion, and HubSpot and you don't need to handle rich text or blog posts, Whalesync is the way to go. Each tool dominates its own turf, so choose based on your core workflow, there's no overlap.

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
Gmail sticks around and gets updates, while Tatem is no longer maintained and its future is uncertain.

Only use Tatem if you have a specific reason, but otherwise use Gmail since at least it will keep working.

Editor's Verdict
Editor's Verdict
Copy Link

Purposeful Design
Purposeful Design
Best
Best

Tatem keeps things simple and distraction-free, so you can tear through your inbox without getting pulled off track. The stripped down design means nothing extra is competing for your attention, and actions like archiving or snoozing are fast. The inbox splits help you process emails in focused batches, making it easy to stay on task.

Gmail, on the other hand, is a cluttered mess for triaging. Visual chaos, pop-ups, and side features like chat and meet make it way too easy to get distracted or sidetracked. Even the label system adds to the noise, and slow load times just invite you to wander off and forget what you were doing.

If you care about staying focused and plowing through email without distractions, Tatem is the obvious pick. Gmail just gets in your way.

Speed & Productivity
Speed & Productivity
Draw

Tatem keeps things quick and distraction-free, letting you fly through emails with fast load times and a clean layout. The basic keyboard shortcuts actually help you stay off the mouse, so processing your inbox feels smooth and direct, even if you miss out on more advanced productivity tricks.

Gmail, on the other hand, feels clunky by comparison. Its shortcuts are scattered and unintuitive, so you end up hunting for commands or giving up and clicking around. Anything beyond the basics, like snoozing, scheduling, or using templates, turns into a tedious, click-heavy process that breaks your flow.

If you want to move fast and get through email without headaches, Tatem is the clear pick. Gmail only makes sense if you absolutely need its extra features and don't mind constant friction. For pure speed and day-to-day productivity, Tatem is just less of a slog.

AI Assistance
AI Assistance
Best
Best

If you want any kind of AI help with your emails, Gmail is the clear choice here. It actually offers AI-powered search and can draft emails for you, even if the writing feels stiff and the workflow is a bit clunky right now. You'll still save some time, especially with its smart search features, as long as you remember where to access them.

Tatem doesn't do any of that. You write and organize everything yourself, with no AI shortcuts or automation. So if saving time with AI matters, don't pick Tatem. Go with Gmail, even if it's not perfect yet.

Follow-Up
Follow-Up
Draw

Neither Gmail nor Tatem gives you any real help with follow-ups. Gmail makes you request read receipts from recipients, but that's clunky and basically useless for tracking follow-ups. Tatem doesn't offer anything at all to help you keep up with replies, so you're stuck remembering everything yourself.

Honestly, both are bad for follow-up. If you care about not missing opportunities, you won't get what you need from either one. There's no real difference here, pick whichever for other reasons, but not for follow-up help.

Team Collaboration
Team Collaboration
Draw

Neither Gmail nor Tatem offers any built-in team collaboration features like commenting, assigning, or sharing. Both force you to handle all teamwork outside the app, making them equally unusable for inbox collaboration.

If you need team collaboration in your email tool, you won't get it from either one. There's no practical difference here, skip both for this need.

### Tatem vs Hey

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
Tatem feels abandoned since the team was acquired by Wander and updates have stopped, while Hey has limitations for business email due to its older SMPT connection.

Stick with Hey only if you need a basic email client and can live with those business limitations, otherwise skip Tatem since it may stop working anytime.

Purposeful Design
Purposeful Design
Best
Best

Tatem gives you a clean, stripped-down inbox that actually lets you triage emails without getting pulled off track. No visual noise, no weird feature names, just fast loading and functional shortcuts that help you clear out your inbox quickly. The split inboxes also make batching a breeze, so you don't get stuck in a scroll hole.

Hey, on the other hand, feels like it's trying too hard to be cute, with oversized icons and playful labels that just slow you down. The interface is distracting, and you can't customize the workflow, so if you care about speed or staying focused, you're always fighting the design.

If your goal is to triage emails fast and stay on task, Tatem is the clear pick. Hey might work if you want something casual and don't mind distractions, but it's not built for anyone who actually wants to move quickly through their inbox.

Speed & Productivity
Speed & Productivity
Draw

Tatem is just noticeably faster and more streamlined for getting through your inbox. It loads quickly, keeps things distraction-free, and actually gives you keyboard shortcuts that work, even if they're simple. That means you can process, archive, and snooze without constantly reaching for the mouse, so you keep your momentum up.

Hey, on the other hand, slows you down if you care about speed. Its clunky interface, oversized icons, and forced structure get in the way, and there's no mention of real productivity boosters like shortcuts or quick replies. You end up doing everything manually, and if you're used to optimizing for speed or customizing your workflow, you'll just feel boxed in and slowed down.

If you want to move fast and process email efficiently, Tatem is the clear pick here. Hey might feel simple if you want the most basic experience, but for real productivity, Tatem keeps you moving.

AI Assistance
AI Assistance
Draw

Neither Hey nor Tatem offers any AI help for writing or organizing your emails. You're stuck doing everything by hand in both, with no smart drafts, sorting, or automation to save you time.

That said, Tatem is slightly less rigid about how you work, while Hey locks you into a fixed inbox structure and manual screening, which might feel even more limiting if you're hoping for anything adaptive.

If you absolutely have to pick between them for AI assistance, Tatem barely edges out Hey, but honestly, you won't get real time savings or writing help from either. If AI is what matters, neither is the answer.

Follow-Up
Follow-Up
Draw

Both Hey and Tatem completely drop the ball on follow-up features. Neither offers smart follow-ups, automations, or even basic reminders, so you're stuck remembering every follow-up by hand and will definitely miss things if you're not on top of it.

There's no edge here. If follow-up speed or reliability matters, neither app is a good pick. You'll run into the same headaches and missed opportunities with both.

Team Collaboration
Team Collaboration
Draw

Neither Hey nor Tatem gives you any way to comment, assign, or share emails with your team. Both force you to handle all collaboration outside the app, so you're stuck with constant context-switching and manual workarounds.

If you need team collaboration in your inbox, neither app fits the bill. There's no practical difference here, both fall flat for team workflows. Pick something else if this matters to you.

Tatem makes it way easier to stay focused and power through your inbox. The stripped down interface keeps distractions out of sight, and everything from loading to basic actions like archiving or snoozing happens fast. You also get inbox splits and working keyboard shortcuts, so you can batch and triage without losing momentum.

Outlook, on the other hand, feels clunky and dated. The traditional layout gets in your way, and there's no real effort to minimize distractions or speed up your workflow. You end up fighting the design instead of getting through your email.

If you care about quickly triaging emails without getting sidetracked, Tatem is the clear choice. Outlook just drags you down. The only reason to pick Outlook here is if you have no other option.

Speed & Productivity
Speed & Productivity
Draw

Tatem is noticeably quicker and more streamlined than Outlook. You get fast load times, responsive actions, and basic but functional keyboard shortcuts to keep your email flow moving. The minimalist design means you aren't bogged down by clutter or lag.

Outlook, on the other hand, feels sluggish and old-school. If you care about moving fast or using shortcuts for rapid triage, you'll keep running into roadblocks. You basically have to use another app just to get to a reasonable workflow speed.

Neither Outlook nor Tatem gives you any help with smart follow-ups. Both leave you stuck tracking and remembering everything yourself, so it's easy to miss replies or let chances slip.

Draw

Both Outlook and Tatem completely miss the mark on team collaboration. Neither lets you comment, assign, or share directly in your inbox, so you're stuck juggling external tools to coordinate with others.

Outlook makes collaboration feel clunky and disconnected, with no way to keep discussions or assignments tied to your messages. Tatem doesn't offer any in-app collaboration either, and flat out says it's unusable if you need to work with others inside your inbox.

Honestly, there's no real difference here, if team collaboration matters, neither one is worth your time.

Best modern async screen recorder for teams

Best modern async screen recorder for teams

Editor's Verdict
Editor's Verdict
Main Category
Main Category
Copy Link

While we use Loom daily (and feel like its a core tool in our business), we are becoming increasingly frustrated with its bloated features (no we don't need AI features 😭 please stop recommending them to us!) and quite slow (and crashes often). That said, we still haven't found an alternative that we are confident to switch to.

We used Tella to record our Motion Course and it was a fantastic tool that allowed us to beautifully record our screen + video and tie it all together with a custom background. That said, we can't envision using Tella in the day-to-day in its current form.

For example, with Loom, a core feature is being able to leave comments at different time stamps. With Tella, while you can leave comments at a time stamp, you have to manually write out the time stamp which is not intuitive.

Further, timestamps stop working after the 1 hour mark in Tella... which look, is probably not commonly needed for a regular Loom-style video, but we did need to it collaborate on for our course video (for internal feedback) so it resulted in a bit of a limiting experience.

Overall, use Loom for daily one-off throw away videos, and Tella for more refined tutorials, courses or videos that you will re-use.

### Tella vs Screen Studio
**Live Link**: [https://efficient.app/compare/tella-vs-screen-studio](https://efficient.app/compare/tella-vs-screen-studio)

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
It's tough to truly compare Tella vs Screen Studio because they are quite different—so different that we actually use both. We used Tella to film our Motion Course as it allowed us the flexibility to record our camera + screen and edit the video all within the tool. On the other hand, we use Screen Studio to show off software in most of our YouTube videos as it allows for beautiful zoom-ins.

Both Tella and Screen Studio have their strengths.

Screen Studio is slightly more high-end for doing screen recordings as they allow for beautiful zoom in's (Tella's zoom feature is much more basic).

If you are doing a high-end tutorial that you want to invest a bit more time into, we'd recommend Screen Studio to make it feel a bit more premium. That said, while Screen Studio does "automatic zooms", they still require some manual effort to get just right so expect to spend a bit of time tinkering with it.

On the other hand, Tella is better if you care less specifically about having fancy zoom-ins on yourscreen recordings, and want to capture multiple elements (like yourself on camera, along with the screen recording, on a nice custom background).

**Live Link**: [https://efficient.app/compare/ticktick-vs-notion](https://efficient.app/compare/ticktick-vs-notion)

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
TickTick keeps things basic and just works if you want a simple and free way to manage tasks, while Notion feels clunky outside of team docs.

Only use Notion if you need a shared team knowledge base; stick with TickTick for straightforward personal task management.

Editor's Verdict
Editor's Verdict
Copy Link

If you just want to quickly jot down personal to-dos or household tasks and need something simple that works right away, TickTick is the clear pick. It's straightforward, requires no setup, and lets you add tasks, notes, priorities, and deadlines with barely any friction. You won't get modern design or instant syncing, but for basic personal task management, it's faster and less hassle.

Notion, on the other hand, is built for teams who want a customizable knowledge base or company wiki. If you try to force it into being a task manager, you'll end up building everything from scratch, wasting time, and probably making things more complicated than they need to be. Most people get bogged down in setup or maintenance, and adoption falls apart unless you're a technical team willing to invest serious effort.

For personal task management, go with TickTick every time. Notion only makes sense if your real need is documentation for a team and you're ready to deal with a complex setup.

### Timely vs Toggl
**Live Link**: [https://efficient.app/compare/timely-vs-toggl](https://efficient.app/compare/timely-vs-toggl)

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
Timely tries to log your activity automatically but just ends up dumping a mess of browser URLs that you have to untangle yourself, while Toggl tracks your time in detail but forces you to do extra manual steps for every entry.

Only use Toggl if you can handle tedious entry work and do not need billing or invoicing, and skip Timely unless you want to sort through confusing logs.

1
Toggl
Recommended
Recommended

Best for tracking what you did all day

Best for tracking what you did all day
Read review
2
Timely

For automatic time tracking

For automatic time tracking
Read review
Trending Right Now
Trending Right Now
Copy Link
1
Superhuman Mail

Best overall email client

Best overall email client
Get 30 days free
Editor's Verdict
Editor's Verdict
Copy Link

Timely tries to automate time tracking by logging your app and website activity, but it mostly just gives you a confusing mess of browser time and endless URLs, leaving you to manually figure out which work was for which client or project. That makes it a pain if you actually need clear, project-specific tracking, you're left sorting through data that doesn't map to real work.

Toggl, on the other hand, asks you to manually enter what you did and for how long, but this process is tedious and adds extra steps every time you log something. It's built more around tracking what you did, not who it was for or what project, so it's not ideal if you need structured, client-focused records. The friction in the workflow makes it hard for teams to build a consistent habit.

Both fail hard if you want effortless or structured time tracking for teams. Timely's automation doesn't deliver usable results and Toggl's manual process is too clunky for regular use. If you're forced to pick, Toggl at least lets you attach entries to projects, but it's still a grind. For anything resembling easy or reliable team time tracking, neither is the right call.

### Todoist vs Linear
**Live Link**: [https://efficient.app/compare/todoist-vs-linear](https://efficient.app/compare/todoist-vs-linear)

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
Todoist covers personal task management simply and reliably, while Linear focuses on project management for engineers and is unmatched there.

Only use Todoist if you want a straightforward tool for your personal life; choose Linear if you need something purpose built for engineering projects.

Editor's Verdict
Editor's Verdict

Linear is built specifically for engineers and product teams, tying together bugs, feature requests, and sprints in a way that makes it the clear favorite for those workflows. If you're in an engineering environment, nothing matches how well Linear fits that process. Teams genuinely enjoy using it, and it's widely advocated by those who rely on it for product management.

Todoist, on the other hand, is practical for personal task management and daily life, but its business and team features are limited. It doesn't offer the same depth for collaboration, project management, or engineering workflows, and even its calendar and time blocking are minimal compared to what's needed for more complex team needs.

If your main goal is managing engineering projects or product cycles, Linear is the obvious pick. Todoist is fine for personal lists, but it just can't compete with Linear's focus and depth for technical teams. If you're not an engineer or don't need that level of project structure, Todoist is simple and straightforward, but for anything serious on the engineering side, Linear is the one to use.

### Todoist vs Morgen Calendar
**Live Link**: [https://efficient.app/compare/todoist-vs-morgen](https://efficient.app/compare/todoist-vs-morgen)

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
Todoist gives you straightforward personal task management with no extras, while Morgen Calendar offers a faster interface and lets you add simple tasks but is mainly a calendar upgrade rather than a true task manager like Todoist.

Only use Morgen Calendar if you want a better calendar and just need basic tasks; stick with Todoist for actual task management.

Editor's Verdict
Editor's Verdict
Main Category
Main Category
Copy Link

Todoist is built specifically for task management and gives you all the basics you'd expect: creating, prioritizing, and organizing tasks with labels, plus list, kanban, and (on paid plans) a simple calendar view. Its focus is clear: if you need to manage tasks, especially for personal life, Todoist does it directly and reliably. While its calendar and time-blocking features are minimal, you still get more task management depth than with Morgen.

Morgen Calendar, on the other hand, is a calendar first. It looks and feels better than Google Calendar or Outlook and lets you add very simple tasks, just a name and deadline, then drag them onto your schedule. But you can't label tasks, categorize them, or manage bigger projects from within Morgen. If all you want is to dabble in daily planning and occasionally toss a task onto your calendar, Morgen feels smooth and easy. But anyone with more than a handful of tasks, or who cares about organizing or tracking them, will quickly hit its limits.

If task management is what matters, Todoist is the clear pick. Morgen only works if your needs are extremely basic and you care more about calendar design than actually managing tasks. For anything beyond dabbling, Todoist simply gives you more control and organizational tools.

**Live Link**: [https://efficient.app/compare/todoist-vs-notion](https://efficient.app/compare/todoist-vs-notion)

Editor's Verdict
Editor's Verdict
Copy Link

Todoist is straightforward for personal task management. You get simple task lists, basic prioritization, and some limited views. If your goal is just to keep track of your own to-dos or personal projects, Todoist does the job without any setup or learning curve.

Notion, on the other hand, tries to be a customizable workspace and can technically act as a task manager if you're willing to build out your own system. But that flexibility turns into a headache for most people. You'll spend more time setting up, maintaining, and explaining your task system than actually using it. Teams especially run into problems with adoption and workflow chaos, since the structure only makes sense to whoever built it.

If you need task management that just works out of the box and doesn't get in the way, Todoist is the clear pick. Notion is only worth considering if you want to build something highly customized and are prepared for ongoing work to keep it usable, but for most people and teams, that quickly becomes more trouble than it's worth.

### Todoist vs Notion Calendar (Cron)
**Live Link**: [https://efficient.app/compare/todoist-vs-notion-calendar](https://efficient.app/compare/todoist-vs-notion-calendar)

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
Todoist keeps personal tasks organized and simple, while Notion Calendar (Cron) only makes sense if you want a free calendar that's nicer than Google Calendar and don't care about Outlook.

Only use Notion Calendar (Cron) if you just need a calendar; stick with Todoist for managing personal tasks, but skip it for business.

Editor's Verdict

Todoist is built around task management, letting you create, prioritize, and organize tasks in a straightforward way. Its calendar view and time blocking are afterthoughts, so if you're relying on it for daily planning or time blocking, you'll end up frustrated or needing to patch things together with other apps.

Notion Calendar (Cron) is all about the calendar experience. It's fast, beautifully designed, and nails the details for scheduling events, but it's not built for managing tasks at all. Trying to use it as a task manager gets messy fast, and even the integration with Notion databases doesn't fix that.

If you want to manage tasks, Todoist actually does the job, even if it's basic. For pure calendar scheduling, Notion Calendar is the clear pick and feels great to use. Don't try to stretch either one into what it isn't. Go with Todoist for tasks, Notion Calendar for events.

### Todoist vs Reclaim AI
**Live Link**: [https://efficient.app/compare/todoist-vs-reclaim](https://efficient.app/compare/todoist-vs-reclaim)

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
Todoist handles basic personal task management and works if you just want to keep track of to-dos, but Reclaim AI is better if you need to block out time for focus and breaks on Google Calendar and mostly deal with internal meetings, not detailed tasks or external scheduling.

Only use Todoist if you want simple personal task lists; choose Reclaim AI if your main problem is defending focus time from internal meetings on Google Calendar.

Editor's Verdict
Editor's Verdict
Main Category

Todoist gives you straightforward task management with lists, priorities, labels, and a couple of basic views. You get exactly what you expect: you can organize your own tasks, see them in a list or kanban, and, if you pay, use a barebones calendar view. Time blocking is possible but feels tacked on and limited. If you want to actually manage a bunch of tasks for yourself, Todoist covers the basics without much fuss, but don't expect advanced planning or anything beyond simple organization.

Reclaim AI, on the other hand, isn't really a task manager. It's built for people constantly stuck in meetings who need to carve out time for focus work and habits on their Google Calendar. You can enter some tasks, but the task features are minimal, just priority and deadline, with no real project management, collaboration, or big-picture task tracking. Reclaim expects you to use a separate task manager for anything beyond that.

If you care about managing tasks, adding them, organizing them, seeing what's next, and actually handling your to-do list, Todoist is the only real option here. Reclaim is for defending calendar space, not managing actual tasks. Go with Todoist if you want to get your tasks under control. Use Reclaim only if your main pain is having no focus time because of nonstop meetings, not if you need to actually manage your work.

### Todoist vs Routine
**Live Link**: [https://efficient.app/compare/todoist-vs-routine](https://efficient.app/compare/todoist-vs-routine)

**Detailed Comparison & Verdict**:
Todoist keeps task management simple and practical for personal use, while Routine gets confusing by trying to do too much with planning and databases.

Only use Routine if you really need its attempt at connecting knowledge and projects, but Todoist is the clear pick for straightforward daily task handling.

Editor's Verdict
Editor's Verdict
Main Category
Main Category
